explanation: 'To Live or Not to Live' is a philosophical and autobiographical work by the renowned Indian writer Nirad C. Chaudhuri, exploring themes of existence and identity.

subject: History
text: In India the first television programme was broadcasted in
explanation: The first experimental television program in India was broadcast on September 15, 1959, from Delhi, marking the beginning of television services in the country.

subject: History
text: Lala Lajpat Rai is the author of the book
explanation: 'Unhappy India' is a significant work by Lala Lajpat Rai, an Indian nationalist and freedom fighter, which critiques Katherine Mayo's 'Mother India'.

subject: History
text: 'A Voice for Freedom' is a book written by
explanation: 'A Voice for Freedom' is an autobiographical work by Nayantara Sahgal, an Indian writer and a prominent voice against the Emergency in India.

subject: History
text: Who is the father of English Poetry?
explanation: Geoffrey Chaucer, known for 'The Canterbury Tales', is widely considered the 'Father of English Poetry' for his pioneering use of English vernacular in his works.

subject: History
text: The popular TV serial 'Neem ka Ped' was written by
explanation: The acclaimed Indian television serial 'Neem ka Ped', which depicted rural life and social issues, was based on a story by the renowned writer Rahi Masoom Raza.

subject: History
text: Who wrote 'War and Peace'?
explanation: 'War and Peace' is an epic novel by the Russian author Leo Tolstoy, renowned for its historical scope and philosophical depth.

subject: History
text: Which of the book was written by Graham Greene?
explanation: 'The Power and the Glory' is a classic novel by Graham Greene, exploring themes of faith, morality, and human weakness in a totalitarian state.

subject: History
text: Former Australian captain Mark Taylor has had several nicknames over his playing career. Which of the following was NOT one of them?
explanation: Mark Taylor was famously known as 'Tubby' and also had nicknames like 'Stodge' and 'Helium Bat'. 'Stumpy' was not one of his known nicknames.

subject: History
text: Which was the 1st non Test playing country to beat India in an international match?
explanation: Sri Lanka got the status of Test playing country in 1981, and beat India in the 1979 World Cup. Before this they were champion of ICC non-test playing countries.

subject: History
text: Track and field star Carl Lewis won how many gold medals at the 1984 Olympic games?
explanation: Lewis has won nine Olympic gold medals in all: four in 1984 (100m, 200m, 4x100m, long jump), two in 1988 (100m and long jump), two in 1992 (4x100m and long jump), and one in 1996 (long jump).

subject: History
text: Which county did Ravi Shastri play for?
explanation: Ravi Shastri played for the English county cricket team Glamorgan during the 1987 season.

subject: History
text: Who was the first Indian to win the World Amateur Billiards title?
explanation: Wilson Jones made history by becoming the first Indian to win the World Amateur Billiards Championship in 1958, a feat he repeated in 1964.

subject: History
text: Who is the first Indian woman to win an Asian Games gold in 400m run?
explanation: Kamaljit Sandhu became the first Indian woman to win an individual gold medal at the Asian Games, achieving this in the 400m race at the 1970 Bangkok Asian Games.

subject: History
text: Which two counties did Kapil Dev play?
explanation: Kapil Dev, the legendary Indian all-rounder, played county cricket for Northamptonshire in 1981 and 1983, and for Worcestershire in 1984.

subject: History
text: When was Amateur Athletics Federation of India established?
explanation: Amateur Athletic Federation of India (AAFI) was formed in 1946 at the initiative of Maharaja Yadvindra Singh, the then President of Indian Olympic Association with Prof. G.D. Sondhi as its first President.

subject: History
text: Who did Stone Cold Steve Austin wrestle at the 1998 edition of "Over the Edge"?
explanation: Stone Cold Steve Austin defended his WWF Championship against Dude Love, one of Mick Foley's personas, at Over the Edge 1998. The match, refereed by Vince McMahon, saw several rule changes but Austin ultimately retained his title.

subject: History
text: Ricky Ponting is also known as what?
explanation: Ricky Ponting is widely known by the nickname 'Punter', which refers to his fondness for betting.

subject: History
text: Which of the following countries won the final of the triangular cricket series held in durban in February 1997?
explanation: South Africa won the final of the Standard Bank International One-Day Series, a triangular cricket series held in Durban in February 1997, defeating India.

subject: History
text: Who has scored the most runs in a single First-class innings?
explanation: Brian Lara holds the record for the highest individual score in first-class cricket, scoring 501 not out for Warwickshire against Durham in 1994.

subject: History
text: How long are professional Golf Tour players allotted per shot?
explanation: Professional golf tours generally allot players 40-45 seconds to make a shot once it is their turn and they are ready to play, to maintain the pace of play.

subject: History
text: Which NBA player scored 8 points in the final 7 seconds of a game to lead his team to victory?
explanation: Reggie Miller achieved this feat in Game 1 of the 1995 Eastern Conference Semifinals against the New York Knicks, scoring 8 points in the final 8.9 seconds to secure a victory for the Indiana Pacers.

subject: History
text: In the match between India and Pakistan at Jaipur on 02-10-1983, Which new rule was introduced?
options: Limit of overs was reduced to 50 overs
The rule of field restriction was taken.
Over throw runs were batsman's score
No-balls & wides were debited to bowlers analysis
explanation: In this match, a new rule was introduced where no-balls and wides were debited to the bowler's analysis, impacting their overall bowling figures. Previously, these were only added to the team's extras.

subject: History
text: The first hang gliders to be flown were flown in...?
explanation: The pioneering work in unpowered flight, which laid the foundation for modern hang gliding, was largely conducted in the late 1800s by figures like Otto Lilienthal.

subject: History
text: Which two counties did Anil Kumble play for?
explanation: Anil Kumble played for two English county cricket clubs: Northamptonshire in 1995, where he had a very successful season, and Leicestershire in 2000.

subject: Science & Tech
text: In which decade was the American Institute of Electrical Engineers (AIEE) founded?
explanation: The American Institute of Electrical Engineers (AIEE) was founded in 1884. It later merged with the Institute of Radio Engineers (IRE) in 1963 to form the modern IEEE.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What is part of a database that holds only one type of information?
explanation: In a database, a field is the smallest unit of information that holds a single type of data, such as a name, age, or product ID. Multiple fields combine to form a record.

subject: Science & Tech
text: 'OS' computer abbreviation usually means ?
explanation: In computing, 'OS' is a widely recognized abbreviation for Operating System, which is software that manages computer hardware and software resources.

subject: Science & Tech
text: In which decade with the first transatlantic radio broadcast occur?
explanation: On December 12, 1901, Guglielmo Marconi successfully received the first transatlantic wireless signal (Morse Code) from Poldhu, Cornwall, in St. John's, Newfoundland.

subject: Science & Tech
text: '.MOV' extension refers usually to what kind of file?
explanation: The '.MOV' file extension is primarily associated with Apple's QuickTime File Format, which is commonly used for storing video (movie) and animation files.

subject: Science & Tech
text: In which decade was the SPICE simulator introduced?
explanation: The SPICE (Simulation Program with Integrated Circuit Emphasis) simulator was introduced in May 1972 by the University of California, Berkeley, becoming a widely used tool for electronic circuit design.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Most modern TV's draw power even if turned off. The circuit the power is used in does what function?
explanation: Even when turned off, modern TVs draw power to keep the remote control receiver circuit active, allowing the device to be powered on remotely. This is often referred to as 'standby power.'

subject: Science & Tech
text: Which is a type of Electrically-Erasable Programmable Read-Only Memory?
explanation: Flash memory is a type of EEPROM that allows data to be erased and reprogrammed in blocks, making it suitable for applications like USB drives, solid-state drives, and mobile devices.

subject: Science & Tech
text: The purpose of choke in tube light is ?
explanation: The choke in a tube light circuit provides a momentary high voltage spike to ionize the gas inside the tube, initiating the discharge. After ignition, it acts as a ballast to limit the current.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Larger buildings may be supplied with a medium voltage electricity supply, and will required a substation or mini-sub. What is the main item of equipment contained in these?
explanation: The main item of equipment in a substation is a transformer, which steps down the medium voltage electricity supply to a lower, usable voltage for the building.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What was the active medium used in the first working laser ever constructed?
explanation: The first working laser, constructed by Theodore Maiman in 1960, utilized a synthetic ruby rod as its active medium to produce a coherent light beam.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What does EPROM stand for?
explanation: EPROM stands for Erasable Programmable Read-Only Memory, a type of non-volatile memory chip that can be erased by exposure to strong ultraviolet light and then reprogrammed.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Who is largely responsible for breaking the German Enigma codes, created a test that provided a foundation for artificial intelligence?
explanation: Alan Turing was a brilliant mathematician and computer scientist who played a crucial role in deciphering the Enigma codes and developed the 'Turing Test,' a concept fundamental to artificial intelligence.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Who developed Yahoo?
explanation: Yahoo! was founded by Stanford University graduate students Jerry Yang and David Filo in January 1994, initially as a web directory called 'Jerry and David's Guide to the World Wide Web.'

subject: Science & Tech
text: Made from a variety of materials, such as carbon, which inhibits the flow of current...?
explanation: A resistor is an electrical component designed to inhibit or resist the flow of electric current, converting electrical energy into heat. They are commonly made from materials like carbon.

subject: Science & Tech
text: The most common format for a home video recorder is VHS. VHS stands for...?
explanation: VHS stands for Video Home System, a popular standard for consumer-level analog video recording on cassette tapes. It was developed by JVC and competed with Sony's Betamax format.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What does VVVF stand for?
explanation: VVVF stands for Variable Voltage Variable Frequency, a control method used to adjust the speed and torque of AC induction motors by varying both the voltage and frequency of the power supply.

subject: History
text: The treaty of Mangalore was signed between
options: the English East India Company and Haidar Ali
the English East India Company and Tipu Sultan
Haidar Ali and the Zamorin of Calicut
the French East India Company and Tipu Sultan
explanation: The Treaty of Mangalore was signed on March 11, 1784, between the English East India Company and Tipu Sultan, concluding the Second Anglo-Mysore War.

subject: History
text: To conquer and annex Peshawar and Punjab, Mahmud of Ghazni defeated
explanation: Mahmud of Ghazni repeatedly defeated the Hindushahi rulers, particularly Jayapala and Anandapala, in a series of battles to conquer and annex regions like Peshawar and Punjab.

subject: History
text: To which professions earlier leaders who struggled for freedom of India mainly belonged?
explanation: Many prominent leaders of the Indian freedom struggle, such as Mahatma Gandhi (lawyer), Bal Gangadhar Tilak (journalist), and Gopal Krishna Gokhale (teacher), came from diverse professional backgrounds. Therefore, 'All of the above' is the most appropriate answer.

subject: History
text: The victories of Karikala are well portrayed in
explanation: Pattinappalai, a long poem from the Sangam literature, vividly describes the military conquests and administrative achievements of Karikala Chola, a prominent early Chola king.

subject: History
text: Todar Mal was associated with
explanation: Raja Todar Mal was the Finance Minister of the Mughal empire during Akbar's reign. He was one of the Navaratnas in Akbar's durbar.

subject: History
text: The title of 'Viceroy' was added to the office of the Governor-General of India for the first time in
explanation: The title of 'Viceroy' was added to the office of the Governor-General of India by the Government of India Act of 1858, following the Indian Rebellion of 1857, signifying direct rule by the British Crown.

subject: History
text: To which of the following dynasties did King Bhoja, a great patron of literature and art, belong?
explanation: King Bhoja, renowned for his patronage of literature and art, was a prominent ruler of the Paramara dynasty, which governed the Malwa region of central India.

subject: History
text: Vikramaditya, a king of Ujjain, started the Vikrama samvat in 58 BC in commemoration of his victory over
explanation: The Vikrama Samvat, a historical Hindu calendar, is traditionally believed to have been founded by King Vikramaditya of Ujjain in 58 BC to commemorate his victory over the Saka invaders.

subject: History
text: Two of the great Mughals wrote their own memories. There were
explanation: The first Mughal emperor Babur wrote his autobiography, the Baburnama, and his great-grandson Jahangir penned his memoirs, the Tuzk-e-Jahangiri, both providing valuable historical insights.

subject: History
text: To which king belongs the Lion capital at Sarnath?
explanation: The Lion Capital at Sarnath, a magnificent sculpture of four lions standing back to back, was erected by Emperor Ashoka in the 3rd century BCE to mark the spot where Buddha first taught the Dharma.

subject: History
text: The use of spinning wheel ( Charkha ) became common during the
explanation: The spinning wheel (charkha) was introduced to India from Persia and became widely adopted during the Delhi Sultanate period, making its use common by the 14th century AD.

subject: History
text: The language of discourses of Gautama Buddha was
explanation: Gautama Buddha primarily delivered his discourses in local Prakrit dialects, with Pali being the most prominent and the language in which the earliest Buddhist scriptures, the Pali Canon, are preserved.

subject: History
text: There were widespread risings against the British in the 1820s. Which one of the following did not revolt in the 1820s?
explanation: The Santhal Rebellion occurred much later, from 1855-1856, whereas the Ahoms, Pagal Panthis, and Ramosis all led significant uprisings against the British during the 1820s.

subject: History
text: Velu Thampi led a revolt against the British in state of
explanation: Velu Thampi Dalawa, the Dewan of Travancore, led a significant rebellion against the British East India Company in 1809, protesting against their interference in the state's internal affairs.

subject: History
text: Under the Mountbatten Plan of 1947 the people of ___ were given the right to decide through a plebiscite whether they wished to join Pakistan or India.
options: N.W.F.P and the Sylhet district of Assam
explanation: The Mountbatten Plan of 1947 stipulated that a plebiscite would be held in the North-West Frontier Province and a referendum in the Sylhet district of Assam to determine their accession to either India or Pakistan.

subject: History
text: Three major powers that emerged in southern India in the 7th century AD were Cheras Cholas Chalukyas Pallavas Pandyas
explanation: In the 7th century AD, the three dominant powers in Southern India were the Chalukyas of Badami, the Pallavas of Kanchi, and the Pandyas of Madurai, who frequently engaged in conflicts for regional supremacy.

subject: History
text: The term 'Yavanapriya' mentioned in ancient Sanskrit texts denoted
options: a fine variety of Indian muslin
damsels sent to the Greek court for dance performance
explanation: The term 'Yavanapriya' literally translates to 'beloved of the Yavanas' (Greeks or foreigners) and was used in ancient Sanskrit texts to refer to pepper, a highly prized spice in trade with the Roman and Greek worlds.

subject: History
text: The Timariots Governors and the Revenue Contractors, on their part reason in this manner: "Why should the neglected state of this land create uneasiness in our minds and why should we expend our money and time to render it fruitful? We may be deprived of it in a single moment, and our exertions would benefit neither ourselves nor our children." This statement was made by
explanation: This observation, highlighting the lack of incentive for land improvement due to insecure tenure, was made by François Bernier, a French traveler and physician who extensively documented his experiences in Mughal India.

subject: History
text: The ultimate ownership of land during the post-Gupta period lay with
explanation: During the post-Gupta period, the king was traditionally considered the ultimate owner of all land, granting him the right to collect revenue and allocate land use, though cultivators held hereditary rights of possession.

subject: History
text: To which of the republic of Buddha belong?
explanation: Gautama Buddha was born into the Shakya clan, which was a small republican state (gana-sangha) located in the foothills of the Himalayas, with its capital at Kapilavastu.

subject: History
text: There was a sharp class division at Harappa and Mohen-jodaro. This is clear from the
options: tools and implements used by the Harappans
explanation: The archaeological evidence of distinct residential areas, such as the fortified citadel and the lower town with varying sizes and qualities of houses, clearly indicates a sharp class division within Harappan and Mohenjo-Daro societies.

subject: History
text: The title given by the British Government to Mahatma Gandhi which he surrendered during the non-cooperation movement was
explanation: Mahatma Gandhi surrendered his Kaiser-e-Hind medal, awarded by the British for his humanitarian work, in 1920 as a protest against British policies and the Jallianwala Bagh massacre, marking the beginning of the Non-Cooperation Movement.

subject: History
text: Tipu sultan was the ruler of
explanation: Tipu Sultan, known as the "Tiger of Mysore," was the formidable ruler of the Kingdom of Mysore in South India, famous for his resistance against the British East India Company.

subject: History
text: The term yavanika meant
explanation: In ancient Indian Sanskrit drama, the term 'Yavanika' referred to a stage curtain, believed to have been introduced to Indian theatre through Greek influence.

subject: History
text: The term Khalisa in Mughal administration signified the
options: land owned b the emperor himself
land from where revenue was collected for the Imperial Treasury
explanation: In Mughal administration, 'Khalisa' referred to lands whose revenue was directly collected by the central government and deposited into the imperial treasury, as opposed to lands assigned as jagirs or religious grants.

subject: History
text: Visakhadatta sketches the event after the death of Samudragupta in his work
explanation: Visakhadatta's play Devi Chandraguptam narrates the events following Samudragupta's death, focusing on the story of Chandragupta II and his elder brother Ramagupta, though only fragments of the play survive.

subject: History
text: The system of Dual Government during the latter half of the 18th century AD is associated with the name of
explanation: The Dual System of Government in Bengal, established by Robert Clive after the Battle of Buxar in 1765, separated the Diwani (revenue collection) and Nizamat (administrative and judicial) functions, with the British controlling the former and the Nawab the latter.

subject: History
text: 'The Vedas contain all the truth was interpreted by
explanation: Swami Dayananda Saraswati, founder of the Arya Samaj, propagated the idea that the Vedas are the fount of all knowledge and truth, urging a return to Vedic principles.

subject: History
text: The term samanta , meaning a feudatory from the sixth century AD, originally meant a
explanation: The term 'samanta' originally meant 'neighbor' or 'bordering' in ancient Indian texts. By the post-Gupta period, its meaning evolved to signify a feudatory chief or tributary ruler.

subject: History
text: To evolve a peaceful settlement of the conflict between India and China, which of the following non-aligned Afro-Asian nations participated in a conference held in December 1962?
options: Burma (now Myanmar), Combodia, Indonesia and UAR
Burma, Sri Lanka, Combodia and Indonesia
Burma, Indonesia, Ghana and Sri Lanka
explanation: The Colombo Powers Conference in December 1962 involved six non-aligned nations – Burma, Cambodia, Ceylon (Sri Lanka), Ghana, Indonesia, and the United Arab Republic (UAR) – to mediate the Sino-Indian border conflict. All nations listed in options A, B, and C were participants.

subject: History
text: The text of the document called Mahzar , by which Akbar assumed the role of supreme arbiter in the matters of religion is found in
explanation: The text of the Mahzar, a decree issued by Akbar in 1579 that gave him supreme authority in religious matters, is primarily found in Badauni's *Muntakhab-ut-Tawarikh*, often accompanied by his critical commentary.

subject: History
text: Tulsidas, the author of Ramcharitmanas , was a contemporary of which of the following rulers?
explanation: Tulsidas, the renowned poet and philosopher, lived from the mid-16th to early 17th century (c. 1532/1543–1623), making him a contemporary of the Mughal emperor Akbar (reigned 1556–1605).

subject: History
text: To meet the educational needs of the people, the Madarasa-I Nasiri was built in the region of
explanation: The Madarasa-i Nasiri, an important educational institution, was built by Sultan Iltutmish in Delhi. He constructed it in memory of his eldest son, Prince Nasiruddin Mahmud.

subject: History
text: The weekly Commonweal was founded by
explanation: The weekly newspaper *Commonweal* was founded by Annie Besant in 1914. She used it to advocate for Home Rule for India and to promote her social and political views.

subject: History
text: Ustad Mansur was a famous painter in the reign of
explanation: Ustad Mansur was a celebrated Mughal painter who flourished during the reign of Emperor Jahangir. He was particularly renowned for his detailed and realistic depictions of flora and fauna.

subject: History
text: The Vedic deity Indra was the Goddess of
explanation: Indra is a prominent Vedic deity, revered as the king of the gods and the god of thunder, storms, and rain. He is also associated with warfare and is a protector of the devas.

subject: History
text: Tolkappiyam is associated with the
explanation: Tolkappiyam is the earliest surviving work of Tamil literature and a treatise on Tamil grammar and poetics. It is traditionally attributed to the Second Sangam period, though its exact dating is debated among scholars.

subject: History
text: Pulakesin II was the most famous ruler of
explanation: Pulakesin II was the most powerful and famous ruler of the Chalukya dynasty of Badami. He is well-known for his military campaigns, including his decisive victory over the North Indian emperor Harsha.

subject: History
text: The term Brahmadeya occurs for the first time in
explanation: The term 'Brahmadeya' refers to land grants made to Brahmins, often tax-free. Its earliest epigraphic occurrences are found in pre-Gupta inscriptions, particularly from the Satavahana and early Pallava periods.

subject: History
text: Under whose leadership was the all India Muslim League set up?
explanation: The All-India Muslim League was founded in Dhaka in 1906 to protect the political rights of Muslims in British India. Aga Khan III was elected as its first permanent president.

subject: History
text: Though Ashoka had many sons, the inscriptions mentioned only one who is not mentioned in any other source. He is
explanation: Among Ashoka's many sons, only Tivara is explicitly mentioned by name in his inscriptions, specifically in the Queen's Edict. Other sons like Mahendra and Kunala are known from Buddhist literary sources.

subject: History
text: We can know about early vedic period from
explanation: The Rig Veda is the most significant and primary source of information for understanding the Early Vedic Period. Its hymns provide insights into the social, religious, economic, and political life of the Rigvedic Aryans.

subject: History
text: The Upanishads are
options: books on social behavior of man
explanation: The Upanishads are a collection of philosophical texts that form the foundational principles of Hinduism. They primarily discuss concepts such as Brahman (the ultimate reality), Atman (the soul), karma, and moksha (liberation).

subject: History
text: Universities in the Presidency towns in India were established in
explanation: The Universities of Calcutta, Bombay, and Madras were established in 1857. This was a direct result of Wood's Despatch of 1854, which recommended the establishment of universities in India on the model of the University of London.

subject: History
text: The Vijayanagara king who employed skilled archers of the Turkish clan and raised the fighting capacity of his bowmen was
explanation: Devaraya I, a ruler of the Vijayanagara Empire, significantly strengthened his army by recruiting skilled Muslim archers and cavalrymen. This move greatly enhanced the fighting capacity of his forces.

subject: History
text: Under the Guptas in eastern India, there was probably an intermediate level of administration between vishayas (districts) and villages. Identify it.
explanation: In the Gupta administrative structure, *bhuktis* were provinces and *vishayas* were districts. The *vithi* served as an intermediate administrative unit, grouping several villages together, positioned between the district and individual villages.

subject: History
text: The two principles monuments of Alaud-din Khilji's reign - the Jama at Kana Masjid and Alai Darwaza - were constructed at
explanation: Both the Alai Darwaza and the Jama'at Khana Masjid are significant architectural contributions from the reign of Alauddin Khalji. The Alai Darwaza is located in the Qutb Complex, and the Jama'at Khana Masjid is in the Nizamuddin Dargah complex, both situated in Delhi.

subject: History
text: The term Nirgrantha is associated with
explanation: The term 'Nirgrantha' literally means 'free from all bonds' or 'without ties.' It was an ancient term used to refer to ascetics, particularly those who followed the teachings that later became known as Jainism.

subject: History
text: The Kalinga was fought in
explanation: The Kalinga War was a major conflict fought by Emperor Ashoka of the Mauryan Empire against the state of Kalinga. It occurred in 261 BCE and is famously known for Ashoka's subsequent conversion to Buddhism due to the immense bloodshed.

subject: History
text: Under the Government of India, Provincial Legislatures consisted of two chambers, except in the case of
explanation: The Government of India Act 1935 established bicameral legislatures (two chambers) in six provinces: Assam, Bengal, Bihar, Bombay, Madras, and the United Provinces. Punjab, along with other provinces like Sindh, NWFP, Orissa, and Central Provinces, had a unicameral legislature.

subject: Geography
text: What is the predominant type of Indian agriculture?
explanation: Subsistence agriculture is the predominant type of farming in India, especially among small and marginal farmers. In this system, farmers primarily cultivate crops and raise livestock to meet the food and other basic needs of their own families.

subject: Geography
text: The Radcliffe line is a boundary between
explanation: The Radcliffe Line is the boundary demarcation line between India and Pakistan. It was drawn in 1947 by Sir Cyril Radcliffe during the partition of British India, dividing the provinces of Punjab and Bengal.

subject: Geography
text: Which of the following has a potential for harnessing of tidal energy in India?
explanation: The Gulf of Cambay (Khambhat) has one of the highest tidal ranges globally, making it a prime location for harnessing tidal energy in India. Other potential sites include the Gulf of Kutch and the Sunderbans.

subject: Geography
text: The typical area of sal forest in the Indian peninsular upland occurs
options: between the Tapti and the Narmada
to the north-east of the Godavari
explanation: Sal forests are a dominant tree species in the moist deciduous forests of eastern and central India. The region to the north-east of the Godavari, including parts of Odisha, Chhattisgarh, and Jharkhand, is well-known for its extensive sal forest cover.

subject: Geography
text: The state having a largest area of forest cover in India is
explanation: Madhya Pradesh consistently holds the distinction of having the largest forest cover area among all Indian states, as per the India State of Forest Reports.

subject: Geography
text: The year ____ is called a Great Divide in the demographic history of India.
explanation: The year 1921 is termed the 'Great Divide' in India's demographic history because it marked the transition from a period of stagnant or negative population growth to one of steady and rapid population increase. This shift was primarily due to a decline in mortality rates.

subject: Geography
text: The only private sector refinery set up by Reliance Petroleum Ltd. is located at
explanation: The Reliance Petroleum Ltd. (now Reliance Industries Limited) refinery, one of the world's largest, is located in Jamnagar, Gujarat. It is a significant private sector player in India's refining industry.

subject: Geography
text: The only state in India that produces saffron is
explanation: Jammu and Kashmir, particularly the Pampore region, is traditionally the only state in India where saffron is cultivated. Its unique climatic conditions are ideal for growing this valuable spice.

subject: Geography
text: Three important rivers of the Indian subcontinent have their sources near the Mansarover Lake in the Great Himalayas. These rivers are
explanation: The Indus, Brahmaputra, and Sutlej rivers all originate in the vicinity of Lake Manasarovar and Rakshastal in the Tibetan Plateau. These trans-Himalayan rivers are crucial to the hydrology of the Indian subcontinent.

subject: Geography
text: The zonal soil type of peninsular India belongs to
explanation: Red soils are the most widespread zonal soil type found across a significant portion of peninsular India, particularly in the Deccan Plateau and Eastern Ghats. They are formed from the weathering of ancient crystalline and metamorphic rocks.

subject: Geography
text: The most plausible explanation for the location of the Thar desert in western India is
options: the obstruction caused by the Aravalis to the rain-bearing wind that proceeds to the Ganga Valley
the evaporation of moisture by heat
the absence of mountains to the north of Rajasthan to cause orographic rainfall in it
that the moisture carried by the South-west monsoon is driven away by the dry upper air current
explanation: The Thar Desert's location is primarily due to the moisture carried by the South-west monsoon being driven away by dry upper air currents, leading to atmospheric subsidence and inhibiting rainfall. Additionally, the Aravalli Range runs parallel to the monsoon winds, preventing significant orographic precipitation.

subject: Geography
text: The northern boundary of the peninsular plateau of Indian runs parallel to the Ganga and the Yamuna from Rajmahal hills to a point near
explanation: The northern boundary of the Indian Peninsular Plateau extends from the Rajmahal Hills in the east, running roughly parallel to the Ganga and Yamuna plains, and reaches close to Delhi in the west, marked by the northern extensions of the Aravalli Range.

subject: Geography
text: Which of the following food grain crops occupies the largest part of the cropped area in India?
explanation: Rice is the most important food grain crop in India, occupying the largest share of the country's total cropped area. It is the staple food for a significant portion of the Indian population.

subject: Geography
text: The number of major languages, recognized in the Indian Union as official language, are
explanation: The Eighth Schedule of the Indian Constitution recognizes 22 languages as official languages. These languages represent the linguistic diversity of the country.

subject: Geography
text: The oldest rocks in India are reported from
explanation: The Dharwar region in Karnataka is home to some of the oldest rock formations in India, belonging to the Archaean age. These ancient rocks are part of the Dharwar Craton, a significant geological feature.

subject: Geography
text: Which of the following groups of rivers originate from the Himachal mountains?
explanation: The Beas, Ravi, and Chenab rivers all originate within the Himachal Pradesh Himalayas. The Beas originates from Beas Kund, the Ravi from Bara Bhangal, and the Chenab is formed by the confluence of the Chandra and Bhaga rivers.

subject: Geography
text: Which of the following groups of states has the largest deposits of iron ore?
explanation: Odisha (formerly Orissa) and Jharkhand (part of erstwhile Bihar) together hold the largest deposits of high-grade iron ore in India. This region is a major hub for iron and steel production.

subject: Geography
text: Which of the following union territories of India has the highest density of population per sq km?
explanation: Delhi, the National Capital Territory, has the highest population density among all Union Territories and states in India. Its urbanized nature and large population contribute to this high density.

subject: Geography
text: which atomic power station in India is built completely indigenously?
explanation: The Madras Atomic Power Station (MAPS) at Kalpakkam in Tamil Nadu is notable for being India's first completely indigenously built nuclear power plant. It was constructed without any foreign collaboration.

subject: Geography
text: The south-west monsoon contributes ____ of the total rain in India.
explanation: The South-West Monsoon is the most significant rainfall season for India, contributing approximately 75-80% of the country's total annual precipitation. This monsoon is crucial for Indian agriculture and water resources.

subject: Geography
text: The Shimla Convention is an agreement that sets
options: Shimla as the capital of Himachal Pradesh
explanation: The Shimla Convention of 1914 was an agreement signed between Great Britain, China, and Tibet, primarily establishing the McMahon Line as the boundary between British India and Tibet. This line remains a point of contention between India and China.

subject: Geography
text: Which of the following events took place in the Cenozoic era?
options: Formation of the rockies, India collides with Asia and the formation of the Himalayas and the Alps
Formation of the Appalachians and central European mountains
explanation: The Cenozoic Era witnessed significant geological events, including the collision of the Indian plate with the Eurasian plate, leading to the formation of the Himalayas. This era also saw the uplift of other major mountain ranges like the Alps and the Rockies.

subject: Geography
text: The oldest oil field in India is the ____ field, in ____
explanation: Digboi in Assam is recognized as the oldest oil field in India, with crude oil first discovered there in 1889. It has been in continuous production since 1901, earning it the title 'Oil City of Assam.'

subject: Geography
text: Unlike other parts of the Indian Coast, fishing industry has not developed along the Saurashtra coast because
options: there are few indentions suitable for fishing
of overwhelming dependence on agriculture and animal husbandary
the sea water is relatively more saline
of industrial development leading to widespread pollution of coastal area
explanation: The premise of the question is factually incorrect, as Gujarat's Saurashtra coast has a well-developed and significant fishing industry. However, if one were to consider factors that might relatively limit its growth compared to other sectors, a historical or economic emphasis on agriculture and animal husbandry could be a contributing factor.

subject: Geography
text: The mountain building in Himalayas began
options: when the continental plates of India and Eurasia converged on each other
explanation: The formation of the Himalayas began approximately 45-55 million years ago, initiated by the collision of the northward-moving Indian continental plate with the Eurasian plate. This ongoing convergence continues to cause the uplift of the mountain range.

subject: Geography
text: The outer Himalayas lie between
options: the lease Himalayas and the Indo Gangetic plain
the foot hills and the Indo Gangetic plain
the greater Himalayas and the lesser Himalayas
explanation: The Outer Himalayas, also known as the Shiwaliks, form the southernmost and lowest range of the Himalayas. They are situated between the Lesser Himalayas to their north and the vast Indo-Gangetic Plain to their south.

subject: Geography
text: Which of the following geographical features have played a great unifying role in strengthening the forces of homogeneity of the Indian people? The expanses of water surrounding the peninsula The Himalayan Mountains The vastness of the country The presence of the Indian ocean
explanation: The Himalayan Mountains and the surrounding expanses of water (Indian Ocean, Arabian Sea, Bay of Bengal) have historically defined India as a distinct geographical and cultural entity, fostering a shared identity. While the country's vastness also contributes to its diversity, these features collectively have played a role in shaping a common civilization and a sense of belonging among its people.

subject: Geography
text: Which of the following drainage systems fall into Bay of Bengal?
explanation: The Ganga, Brahmaputra, Godavari, Mahanadi, Krishna, and Cauvery are all major rivers in India that drain into the Bay of Bengal on the eastern coast.

subject: Geography
text: The oldest oil refinery in India is at
explanation: The Digboi Refinery in Assam, established in 1901, holds the distinction of being the oldest operational oil refinery in India.

subject: Geography
text: The oldest mountains in India are
explanation: The Aravalli Range, located in Western India, is considered the oldest fold mountain range in India and one of the oldest in the world.

subject: Geography
text: Which of the following groups of rivers have their source of origin in Tibet?
explanation: The Brahmaputra, Indus, and Sutlej rivers all originate in the Tibetan Plateau, flowing through various countries before reaching their destinations.

subject: Geography
text: Which of the following measures are effective for soil conservation in India? Avoiding crop rotation Afforestation Encouraging the use of chemical fertilizers Limiting shifting cultivation
explanation: Afforestation helps bind soil with roots and reduce erosion, while limiting shifting cultivation prevents deforestation and subsequent soil degradation, both crucial for soil conservation.

subject: Geography
text: Which of the following crops needs maximum water per hectare?
explanation: Sugarcane is a highly water-intensive crop, requiring a substantial amount of water throughout its long growing period compared to other listed crops.

subject: Geography
text: The watershed between India and Myanmar is formed by
explanation: The Naga Hills, part of the Patkai range, form a natural geographical barrier and watershed along the border between India and Myanmar.

subject: Geography
text: The originating in the Himalayan mountain complex consists of how many distinct drainage systems of the Indian Subcontinent?
explanation: The Himalayan mountain complex gives rise to three major distinct drainage systems of the Indian Subcontinent: the Indus, the Ganga, and the Brahmaputra river systems.

subject: Geography
text: The percentage of India's total population employed in agriculture is nearly
explanation: FIX: The correct option was changed from 3 (70%) to 0 (40%). As per recent data (e.g., 2021-22), approximately 40-45% of India's total workforce is employed in agriculture, a figure that has steadily declined over the decades.

subject: Geography
text: Which of the following important rivers of India does not originate from the Western Ghats?
explanation: The Mahanadi River originates in the Sihawa mountains of Chhattisgarh, unlike the Cauvery, Godavari, and Krishna rivers which all have their sources in the Western Ghats.

subject: Geography
text: Which of the following areas or regions is most prone to earthquakes?
explanation: The Ganga-Brahmaputra valley, being part of the highly active seismic zone along the Himalayan front, is significantly more prone to earthquakes than the stable Deccan plateau or Western Ghats.

subject: Geography
text: The proportion of forest to the total national geographical area of India as envisaged by National Forest Policy is
explanation: The National Forest Policy of India aims to maintain 33.3% (one-third) of the country's total geographical area under forest and tree cover for ecological stability.

subject: Geography
text: Which of the following dams has generations of power more than irrigation as its main purpose?
explanation: The Gandhi Sagar Dam, part of the Chambal Valley Project, was primarily designed for significant hydroelectric power generation, alongside irrigation and flood control.

subject: Geography
text: Which of the following crops is regarded as a plantation crop?
explanation: Coconut is considered a plantation crop, typically grown on large estates for commercial purposes, unlike field crops such as cotton, sugarcane, and rice.

subject: Geography
text: Which of the following countries leads in the production of aluminium and its products in the world?
explanation: Historically, the USA was a leading producer of aluminium. However, currently, China is the world's largest producer of primary aluminium, which is not among the options.

subject: Geography
text: The natural region which holds the Indian subcontinent is
explanation: The Indian subcontinent is primarily characterized by a monsoon climate, which brings distinct wet and dry seasons and significantly influences its agriculture and ecosystems.

subject: Geography
text: The most ideal region for the cultivation of cotton in India is
explanation: The Deccan Plateau, particularly its black soil (Regur) regions, provides the most ideal conditions for cotton cultivation in India due to its fertility and moisture-retention properties.

subject: Geography
text: Which of the following are true with respect to the Indian Peninsular Plateau? The southern plateau block is formed mainly of granite and gneiss The Deccan lava plateau is an elevated tableland consisting of horizontally arranged lava sheets The Malwa plateau dominates the Vindhyam scraps, forming the northern flank of the plateau The trough of the Narmada and Tapti are interposed between the Vindhyan and the Satpura ranges
explanation: All four statements accurately describe features of the Indian Peninsular Plateau: its ancient granitic and gneissic southern block, the layered Deccan lava plateau, the Malwa plateau's position relative to the Vindhyas, and the rift valleys of the Narmada and Tapti rivers.

subject: Geography
text: Which of the following types of soil are mostly confined to river basins and coastal plains of India?
explanation: Alluvial soils are formed by the deposition of sediments carried by rivers, making them predominantly found in river basins and coastal plains across India.

subject: Geography
text: The two states of India, most richly endowed with iron ore, are
explanation: Odisha (formerly Orissa) and Jharkhand (historically part of Bihar) are among the states most richly endowed with significant iron ore reserves in India.

subject: Geography
text: The most fertile region of India is
explanation: The Indo-Gangetic Plain is renowned as the most fertile region of India due to its vast expanse of rich alluvial soils deposited by major river systems.

subject: Geography
text: Which of the following groups accounts for over 90 per cent of India's annual coal production?
options: Orissa, Madhya Pradesh and Tamil Nadu
West Bengal, Madhya Pradesh and Tamil Nadu
explanation: Historically, the states of Bihar (including present-day Jharkhand), Odisha, and West Bengal have been the primary coal-producing regions in India, though the claim of 'over 90%' is an exaggeration; these states collectively account for a significant majority.

subject: Geography
text: The significant shifts in Indian agriculture during green revolution include
options: non-food grains reported some spectacular progress and shift in the cropping pattern
major food grains, in the eastern regions productions decreased and in the northern states increased
explanation: FIX: The correct option was changed from 2 (both (a) and (b)) to 1 (major food grains, in the eastern regions productions decreased and in the northern states increased). The Green Revolution primarily focused on major food grains like wheat and rice, leading to increased production in northern states and regional disparities, while non-food grains generally did not see spectacular progress.

subject: Geography
text: The number of major ports in India is
explanation: India currently has 13 major ports, which are managed by the central government and handle a significant volume of the country's maritime trade.

subject: Geography
text: Which of the following is a peninsular river of India?
explanation: The Krishna River originates in the Western Ghats and flows eastward across the Deccan Plateau, making it a prominent peninsular river of India, unlike the Himalayan rivers Gandak, Kosi, and Sutlej.

subject: Geography
text: Which of the following is the most important raw material for generation of power in India?
explanation: Coal is the most important raw material for power generation in India, accounting for the largest share of the country's electricity production, primarily through thermal power plants.

subject: Geography
text: When it is noon IST at Allahabad in India, the time at Greenwich, London, will be
explanation: Indian Standard Time (IST) is 5 hours and 30 minutes ahead of Greenwich Mean Time (GMT). Therefore, if it is 12:00 noon IST, the time at Greenwich will be 12:00 - 5:30 = 06:30 GMT.

subject: Geography
text: Which country has the largest coast line?
explanation: Canada has the longest coastline in the world, stretching over 243,042 kilometers, including its mainland and numerous islands.

subject: Polity
text: The members of the Rajya Sabha are
options: directly elected by the people on the basis of universal adult franchise
elected by the members of the state legislative assemblies
elected by the members of the state legislative councils
elected by the members of the state legislative councils and state legislative assemblies
explanation: Members of Rajya Sabha are elected by the elected members of the Assemblies of States and Union territories in accordance with the system of proportional representation by means of the single transferable vote.

subject: Polity
text: The president addresses both the Houses of Parliament assembled together
options: during emergency session summoned for the purpose
first session after each general election and the first session of each year
explanation: As per Article 87 of the Indian Constitution, the President addresses both Houses of Parliament assembled together at the commencement of the first session after each general election and at the commencement of the first session of each year.

subject: Polity
text: The president can dissolve the Lok Sabha on
options: advice of the chief justice of India
explanation: The President of India can dissolve the Lok Sabha (House of the People) on the advice of the Prime Minister, who leads the Council of Ministers and holds the confidence of the House.

subject: Polity
text: The president can expend out of the Contingency Fund of India
options: only with the approval of the Parliament
without the approval of the Parliament
only in the case of the national calamities
explanation: The President can make advances from the Contingency Fund of India to meet unforeseen expenditures without prior parliamentary approval. However, parliamentary approval is subsequently required to regularize the expenditure and replenish the fund.

subject: Polity
text: The pre-requisite for the enforcement of directive principles of the state policy is
explanation: The enforcement and implementation of Directive Principles of State Policy largely depend on the availability of adequate financial and administrative resources of the state. These principles are non-justiciable, meaning their realization is contingent on the state's capacity.

subject: Polity
text: The Objectives Resolution which laid down the main objectives to guide the deliberations of the Assembly was moved by
explanation: Jawaharlal Nehru moved the historic 'Objectives Resolution' in the Constituent Assembly on December 13, 1946, which laid down the fundamental principles and philosophy that would guide the drafting of the Indian Constitution.

subject: Polity
text: The president can advance money to meet unforeseen expenses, pending authorization by Parliament, from
explanation: The President can advance money from the Contingency Fund of India to meet unforeseen expenses, with the understanding that parliamentary authorization will be sought later to regularize the expenditure and replenish the fund.

subject: Polity
text: The position of a chief minister is
options: similar to that of the prime minister
identical to that of the president
a combination of the position of the prime minister and president
explanation: The position of a Chief Minister in a state is analogous to that of the Prime Minister at the central level, both being the real executive heads of their respective governments in a parliamentary system.

subject: Polity
text: The office of the president can fall vacant due to
explanation: The office of the President can fall vacant due to several reasons, including resignation, death, removal through impeachment, or the expiry of their five-year term.

subject: Polity
text: The president can dismiss a member of the council of ministers
options: on the recommendation of the prime ministers
with the consent of the speaker
explanation: The President can dismiss a member of the Council of Ministers only on the recommendation of the Prime Minister, as the Prime Minister is the head of the Council and holds the authority to recommend appointments and removals.

subject: Polity
text: The president demand for further reforms, attended with the dislocation caused by the non-cooperation movement, led the British government to appoint a Statutory Commission in 1927. This commission was headed by
explanation: The Statutory Commission appointed in 1927 to review constitutional reforms in India was known as the Simon Commission, and it was headed by Sir John Simon.

subject: Polity
text: The office of the prime minister of India
explanation: The office of the Prime Minister of India has a constitutional basis, as Article 75 of the Indian Constitution explicitly states that the Prime Minister shall be appointed by the President.

subject: Polity
text: The powers to legislate with respect to any matter not enumerated in any of the three lists are mentioned as residuary powers. Which of the following is empowered to determine finally as to whether or not a particular matter falls in this category
explanation: While Parliament has the power to legislate on residuary matters, the ultimate authority to determine whether a particular subject falls under the residuary category, through constitutional interpretation and judicial review, rests with the Judiciary.

subject: Polity
text: The members of the parliamentary committee
options: are appointed by the president in consultation with the prime minister
are appointed by the president on the recommendation of the speaker
are taken from various groups and parties in Parliament in proportion to their respective strength
explanation: Members of parliamentary committees are typically selected from various political parties and groups within Parliament in proportion to their respective strengths, ensuring broad representation and reflecting the composition of the House.

subject: Polity
text: The name of a candidate for the office of president of India may be proposed by
options: any five members of the Parliament
50 members of electoral college and supported by another 50 members.
ten members of the Electoral College
explanation: A candidate for the office of President of India must have their nomination proposed by at least 50 electors from the Electoral College and supported by another 50 electors from the same body.

subject: Polity
text: The Parliament can restrict or abrogate by law, fundamental rights with respect to
options: the members of the armed forces
the forces charged with the maintenance of public order
the persons employed in any bureau or other organization established by the state for purpose of intelligence
explanation: Article 33 of the Indian Constitution grants Parliament the power to restrict or abrogate fundamental rights for members of the armed forces, forces maintaining public order, and intelligence personnel, to ensure the proper discharge of their duties and maintenance of discipline.

subject: Polity
text: Preamble enshrines the ideals of liberty, equality and fraternity - ideals inspired by the
explanation: The ideals of liberty, equality, and fraternity, which are enshrined in the Preamble of the Indian Constitution, were profoundly inspired by the principles of the French Revolution.

subject: Polity
text: The minimum number of members that must be present to hold the meeting of the Lok Sabha is
options: one-tenth of the total membership of the Houses
50 percent strength of the Lok Sabha
explanation: As per Article 100(3) of the Indian Constitution, the quorum required to hold a meeting of the Lok Sabha is one-tenth of the total membership of the House.

subject: Polity
text: The Parliament enjoys legislative power over subjects in
options: all the three lists, viz. union list, state list and the concurrent list
explanation: Parliament enjoys legislative power over subjects enumerated in the Union List (exclusively) and the Concurrent List (concurrently with state legislatures), with parliamentary law prevailing in case of conflict.

subject: Polity
text: The power to prorogue the Lok Sabha rests with
explanation: The power to prorogue the Lok Sabha, which signifies the termination of a session of Parliament, rests with the President of India, as per Article 85(2)(a) of the Constitution.

subject: Polity
text: The phrase 'bicameral legislature' means
options: a legislature consisting of a lower and an upper chamber
explanation: A 'bicameral legislature' refers to a legislative body that is composed of two separate chambers or houses, such as the Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha in the Indian Parliament.

subject: Polity
text: The position of the president which was undermined by the 42nd amendment was sub-sequently somewhat retrieved by the
explanation: The 42nd Amendment Act made the President bound by the advice of the Council of Ministers, but the 44th Amendment Act subsequently allowed the President to send the advice back for reconsideration, thereby somewhat retrieving the President's discretionary power.

subject: Polity
text: The oath of office is administered to the members of state council of ministers by
options: chief justice of the state high court
explanation: According to Article 164(3) of the Indian Constitution, the Governor administers the oaths of office and secrecy to the members of the state council of ministers.

subject: Polity
text: The power of Supreme Court of India to decide the dispute between the centre and states falls under its
explanation: The Supreme Court's original jurisdiction, as defined in Article 131 of the Indian Constitution, covers disputes between the Centre and one or more States, or between multiple States.

subject: Polity
text: The members of Lok Sabha are
explanation: Members of the Lok Sabha are directly elected by the people through adult suffrage from territorial constituencies in the states and Union Territories. The 104th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2019, removed the provision for nominated Anglo-Indian members.

subject: Polity
text: The parliament can legislate on a subject in the state list
options: if the Rajya Sabha passes a resolution by two-third majority declaring the subject in state list of national importance
if the legislatures of two or more states recommend to the Parliament to legislate on such a subject with regard to those states
for the implementation of treaties and agreements with foreign powers
explanation: Parliament can legislate on a State List subject under several circumstances, including when the Rajya Sabha passes a resolution of national interest (Art. 249), when two or more states request it (Art. 252), or for implementing international treaties (Art. 253).

subject: Polity
text: The oath of office is administered to the Governor by the
explanation: As per Article 159 of the Indian Constitution, the oath of office to the Governor is administered by the Chief Justice of the High Court of the concerned state, or in their absence, the senior-most judge of that court.

subject: Polity
text: The members of Parliament can express themselves in the House in
explanation: While the business of Parliament is primarily conducted in Hindi or English, members are permitted to address the House in their mother tongue with the prior permission of the presiding officer.

subject: Polity
text: The nature of the anti-Imperialist struggle was
options: initially constitutional and by large non-violent
explanation: The anti-imperialist struggle in India began with constitutional methods by early nationalists, evolving into a largely non-violent mass movement under Mahatma Gandhi, though revolutionary armed resistance also existed.

subject: Polity
text: The position of the prime minister of India is superior to that of his counter-part in Britain because
options: India has adopted the federal system
explanation: The office of the Prime Minister in India is explicitly established and defined by the written Constitution (Articles 74 and 75), providing it with a clear constitutional basis, unlike the UK where the office largely evolved through conventions.

subject: Polity
text: The members of the committees of Parliament are
options: nominated by the leaders of the various parties in the Parliament
appointed by the speaker or elected by the House from amongst persons who are not members of Parliament
appointed by the speaker or elected by the House from amongst its own members
explanation: Members of parliamentary committees are typically appointed by the Speaker (for Lok Sabha) or Chairman (for Rajya Sabha) or elected by the respective House from among its own members.

subject: Polity
text: The national flag was adopted by the Constituent Assembly of India on 22 July 1947 and was presented to the nation at the midnight session of the Assembly on 14th August 1947 on behalf of
explanation: The National Flag of India was adopted by the Constituent Assembly on the 22nd July, 1947 and presented to the Nation on behalf of the women of India at the mid-night session of the assembly on August 14, 1947.

subject: Polity
text: The president convenes and prorogues all sessions of Parliament in consultation with
options: the prime minister and the leader of the Opposition in Lok Sabha
explanation: The President formally summons and prorogues sessions of Parliament, but this action is taken on the aid and advice of the Council of Ministers, which is headed by the Prime Minister.

subject: Polity
text: The preamble to our constitution provided that India is
options: a sovereign, socialist and democratic republic
a sovereign, socialist, secular and democratic republic
a sovereign republic with a socialist pattern of society
a socialist, secular and democratic republic
explanation: The Preamble to the Indian Constitution, as amended in 1976, declares India to be a Sovereign, Socialist, Secular, Democratic Republic.

subject: Polity
text: The minimum age required to become a member of Rajya Sabha is
explanation: To become a member of the Rajya Sabha (Council of States), a person must be at least 30 years of age, as stipulated by Article 84(b) of the Indian Constitution.

subject: Polity
text: The president can be impeached for
options: for not taking the prime minister's advice
explanation: The President of India can be impeached only for 'violation of the Constitution,' as explicitly stated in Article 61 of the Indian Constitution.

subject: Polity
text: The name of the union given in the Constitution is
explanation: Article 1(1) of the Indian Constitution explicitly states the name of the country as 'India, that is Bharat,' signifying both names for the Union.

subject: Polity
text: The phrase 'procedure established by the law' means
options: the judges in India cannot question the fairness or validity of a law, provided it is within the limits of the constitution
judges in India can question the fairness or validity of an undue law even if it is within the limits of the constitution
judges in India can declare a law invalid simply because in their opinion the law is not due or is unjust
explanation: The phrase 'procedure established by law' implies that a law is valid if it has been enacted by the legislature following the prescribed procedure, and the judiciary primarily checks for procedural adherence rather than the substantive fairness or reasonableness of the law itself.

subject: Polity
text: The national anthem was written by
explanation: The National Anthem of India, 'Jana Gana Mana,' was originally composed in Bengali by the renowned poet and polymath Rabindranath Tagore.

subject: Polity
text: The member of a State Public Service Commission can be removed on the ground of misbehavior only after an enquiry has been conducted by the
explanation: A member of a State Public Service Commission can be removed by the President on grounds of misbehavior only after the Supreme Court of India has conducted an inquiry and recommended their removal.

subject: Polity
text: The members of the standing committee are taken from the Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha in the ratio of
options: equal members for both the Houses
explanation: Departmental Standing Committees of the Indian Parliament typically comprise 31 members, with 21 drawn from the Lok Sabha and 10 from the Rajya Sabha, maintaining a 2:1 ratio.

subject: Polity
text: The preamble enshrines certain ideals that were first spelt out in
options: the speech by Jawaharlal Nehru on the banks of Ravi when he called for Purana Swaraj
a resolution adopted at the Karachi session of the Indian National Congress
the Objectives Resolution adopted by the Constituent Assembly
explanation: The ideals enshrined in the Preamble to the Indian Constitution are directly based on the 'Objectives Resolution,' which was moved by Jawaharlal Nehru and adopted by the Constituent Assembly on January 22, 1947.

subject: Polity
text: The Nagaland State of India cannot be termed as a state because it lacks
options: a high court exclusively of its own
explanation: Nagaland is indeed a state within the Indian Union. However, if the question implies why it cannot be considered an independent nation-state, then it lacks sovereignty, as sovereignty in India rests with the Union, not its individual states.

subject: Polity
text: The Objectives Resolution was unanimously adopted by the Constituent Assembly on
explanation: The Objectives Resolution, which laid down the fundamental principles and philosophy of the Indian Constitution, was unanimously adopted by the Constituent Assembly on January 22, 1947.

subject: Polity
text: The preamble declares India as a sovereign state which implies
options: India is free to conduct her internal affairs
India is free to conduct her external affairs
India is free to conduct here internal as well as external affairs
explanation: Declaring India as a sovereign state in the Preamble signifies that India is an independent nation, free from any external control, and has the supreme authority to govern both its internal and external affairs.

subject: Polity
text: The parliament works through committees which
options: help the government in the formulation of policy
exercise effective control over government on a regular and continuing basis
ensure that the parliament strictly adheres to the provision of the constitution and the prescribed parliamentary procedure
explanation: Parliamentary committees are crucial for exercising legislative oversight and accountability over the executive. They scrutinize government policies, bills, and expenditures, thereby ensuring continuous and effective control over the administration.

subject: Polity
text: The Muslim League as a political party was founded in
explanation: The All-India Muslim League, a significant political party during the Indian independence movement, was founded in Dhaka on December 30, 1906, with the aim of protecting the rights of Indian Muslims.

subject: Polity
text: The members of the state legislature exercise control over the council of the ministers through
explanation: Members of the state legislature use various parliamentary tools like questions, supplementary questions, criticism of policies, and adjournment motions to hold the Council of Ministers accountable and exercise control over its functioning.

subject: Polity
text: The preamble to our constitution includes all the following except
explanation: The Preamble to the Indian Constitution outlines the ideals of Justice, Liberty, Equality, and Fraternity. Adult franchise, while a fundamental aspect of Indian democracy, is not explicitly stated in the Preamble.

subject: Polity
text: Mainstream Nationalism in India
options: aimed at restoration of the Hindu state
had national socialism as its ultimate goal
aimed at emancipation from colonial rule
explanation: Mainstream nationalism in India, led by figures like Mahatma Gandhi and Jawaharlal Nehru, primarily aimed at achieving complete independence and emancipation from British colonial rule.

subject: Polity
text: The minimum age required to become the prime minister of India is
explanation: To become the Prime Minister of India, a person must be a member of either the Lok Sabha or the Rajya Sabha. The minimum age to be a member of the Lok Sabha is 25 years, making it the minimum age requirement for the Prime Minister.

subject: Polity
text: The objective of the Morley-Minto Reforms was
options: to give more powers to local government
to abolish the post of secretary of the state for India
explanation: The Morley-Minto Reforms (Indian Councils Act 1909) aimed to expand the size and functions of the legislative councils, both at the central and provincial levels, and introduced separate electorates for Muslims.

subject: Polity
text: The oath of office is conducted to the president by
explanation: According to Article 60 of the Indian Constitution, the oath of office to the President of India is administered by the Chief Justice of India, or in their absence, the senior-most judge of the Supreme Court.

subject: Polity
text: The members of the state legislative assemblies are elected for a period of
explanation: Members of the State Legislative Assemblies (Vidhan Sabha) are elected for a fixed term of five years, unless the assembly is dissolved prematurely.

subject: Polity
text: The president can be removed from his office before the expiry of his normal term only on the recommendation of
explanation: The President of India can be removed from office before the expiry of their term only through an impeachment process, which requires a resolution passed by a special majority in both Houses of Parliament.

subject: Polity
text: The new committee system constitutes an improvement over the earlier committee system in so far as
options: it assures representation to all the political parties in proportion to their strength in the Parliament
it enables the Parliament to examine the grants of all the ministries and departments in detail
it enables the Parliament to accept the demands of various ministries without scrutiny
explanation: The new committee system, particularly the Departmentally Related Standing Committees, allows Parliament to conduct a more detailed and in-depth examination of the demands for grants and legislative proposals of all ministries and departments.

subject: Polity
text: The members of the State Public Service Commission are appointed by the
explanation: As per Article 316 of the Indian Constitution, the Chairman and other members of a State Public Service Commission are appointed by the Governor of the respective state.

subject: Economy
text: Gross domestic capital formation is defined as
options: flow of expenditure devoted to increased or maintaining of the capital stock
expenditure incurred on physical assets only
net addition to stock after depreciation
explanation: Gross domestic capital formation refers to the total expenditure incurred on increasing or maintaining the capital stock of an economy, including fixed assets and changes in inventories, before accounting for depreciation.

subject: Economy
text: On July 12, 1982, the ARDC was merged into
explanation: On July 12, 1982, the Agricultural Refinance and Development Corporation (ARDC) was merged into the newly established National Bank for Agriculture and Rural Development (NABARD) to streamline rural credit.

subject: Economy
text: Which of the following is the most appropriate cause of exports surplus?
options: Developments in national and international markets
explanation: An export surplus is primarily driven by favorable developments in both national and international markets, including strong domestic production, competitive pricing, high global demand for a country's goods, and favorable exchange rates.

subject: Economy
text: If the cash reserve ratio is lowered by the RBI, its impact on credit creation will be to
explanation: When the Reserve Bank of India lowers the Cash Reserve Ratio (CRR), banks are required to hold less money as reserves, freeing up more funds for lending and consequently increasing credit creation in the economy.

subject: Economy
text: Which of the following items would not appear in a company's balance sheet?
options: Value of stocks of raw materials held
Revenue from sales of the company's products
explanation: Revenue from sales is reported on a company's income statement, which summarizes financial performance over a period, whereas a balance sheet presents assets, liabilities, and equity at a specific point in time.

subject: Economy
text: The currency convertibility concept in its original form originated in
explanation: The concept of currency convertibility, particularly for current account transactions, originated with the Bretton Woods Agreement in 1944, which established a post-World War II international monetary system.

subject: Economy
text: In the state of India, the State Financial Corporation have given assistance mainly to develop
explanation: State Financial Corporations (SFCs) in India were primarily established to provide financial assistance and support for the development and growth of medium and small-scale industries within their states.

subject: Economy
text: The central co-operative banks are in direct touch with
explanation: Central Co-operative Banks (CCBs) operate at the district level and are in direct touch with State Co-operative Banks (SCBs) at the apex level and Primary Agricultural Credit Societies (PACS) at the grassroots level.

subject: Economy
text: The first wholly Indian Bank was set up in
explanation: The first wholly Indian bank, Punjab National Bank, was established in 1894, marking a significant step in the development of indigenous banking in India.

subject: Economy
text: States earn maximum revenue through
explanation: State governments in India derive the maximum portion of their own tax revenue from commercial taxes, which include State GST, sales tax on certain goods, stamp duties, and motor vehicle taxes.

subject: Economy
text: Our financial system has provided for the transfer of resources from the centre to the states; the important means of resource transfer are
explanation: The Indian financial system ensures the transfer of resources from the Centre to the States through multiple channels, including the sharing of central taxes, various grants-in-aid, and loans.

subject: Economy
text: Debenture holders of a company are its
explanation: Debenture holders are individuals or entities who have lent money to a company by purchasing its debentures, making them creditors of the company rather than shareholders.

subject: Economy
text: Excise duty is a tax levied on the
explanation: Excise duty is an indirect tax levied on the manufacture or production of goods within a country, distinguishing it from sales tax which is levied on the sale of goods.

subject: Economy
text: In pursuance with the recommendations of Narsimhan Committee, the RBI has framed new guidelines
options: to govern entry of new private sector banks to make the banking sector more competitive
to reduce the freedom given to banks to rationalize their existing branch network
to setup more foreign exchange banks
to lend more easily for industrial development
explanation: Following the recommendations of the Narasimham Committee, the RBI framed new guidelines to facilitate the entry of new private sector banks, aiming to foster greater competition and efficiency within the Indian banking sector.

subject: Economy
text: Which of the following is the first Indian private company to sign an accord with Government of Myanmar for oil exploration in two offshore blocks in that country?
explanation: Essar Oil was among the first Indian private companies to sign an accord with the Government of Myanmar for oil and gas exploration in offshore blocks, marking its entry into international upstream operations.

subject: Economy
text: Non Tax revenues can be increased by improving the working of the
explanation: Improving the efficiency and profitability of public sector undertakings like State Road Transport Corporations, electricity boards, and commercial irrigation projects directly increases the non-tax revenue generated by the government.

subject: Economy
text: Which of the following is not viewed as a national debt?
explanation: Provident Funds, National Saving Certificates, and Long-term Government Bonds represent direct borrowings by the government from the public, forming part of the national debt. Life Insurance Policies are contracts with insurance companies and do not directly constitute national debt.

subject: Economy
text: The condition of indirect taxes in the country's revenue is approximately
explanation: Historically, particularly in the late 1980s and early 1990s, indirect taxes constituted a very large proportion, sometimes exceeding 80%, of the central government's total tax revenue in India.

subject: Economy
text: Deficit financing means that the government borrows money from the
explanation: Deficit financing typically involves the government borrowing from the central bank (like the RBI) to cover its expenditure when revenue falls short, often leading to an increase in the money supply.

subject: Economy
text: Revenue of the state governments are raised from the following sources, except
explanation: State governments derive revenue from sources like entertainment tax, agricultural income tax, and land revenue. Expenditure tax, however, was historically a central government tax and is not a source of revenue for state governments.

subject: Economy
text: Since the inception of the co-operative movement, rural credits has been
explanation: The cooperative movement in India aimed to institutionalize rural credit, rationalize lending procedures, and make credit more affordable for farmers and rural communities.

subject: Economy
text: If an economy is equilibrium at the point where plans to save and to invest are equal, then government expenditure must be
explanation: In a three-sector economy, the equilibrium condition is S + T = I + G. If plans to save (S) and invest (I) are equal (S=I), then for the economy to be in equilibrium, government expenditure (G) must be equal to government income (T), implying a balanced budget.

subject: Economy
text: The co-operative credit societies have a
explanation: Cooperative credit societies in India typically operate on a three-tier structure, comprising Primary Agricultural Credit Societies at the village level, District Central Cooperative Banks at the district level, and State Cooperative Banks at the state level.

subject: Economy
text: Regional rural banks have limited area of operation have free access to liberal refinance facilities from NABARD are required to lend only to weaker sections
explanation: Regional Rural Banks (RRBs) operate within a limited geographical area and receive refinance facilities from NABARD. While they primarily focus on lending to weaker sections, they are not exclusively restricted to lending *only* to them.

subject: Economy
text: The Board of Industrial and Financial Reconstruction (BIFR) came into existence in
explanation: The Board of Industrial and Financial Reconstruction (BIFR) was constituted in January 1987 under the Sick Industrial Companies (Special Provisions) Act, 1985 (SICA), to revive sick industrial companies.

subject: Economy
text: The current price index (base 1960) is nearly 330. This means that
options: all items cost 3-3 times more than what they did in 1960
the prices of certain selected items have gone up to 3-3 times
weighted means of prices of certain item has increased 3-3 times
gold price has gone up 3-3 times
explanation: A price index of 330 (with a base of 100 in 1960) indicates that the weighted average of prices for a specific basket of goods and services has increased by 3.3 times (or 230%) compared to the base year 1960.

subject: Economy
text: Notes on which denomination has the portrait of Mahatma Gandhi printed on them?
