text: The main chemical constituent of clay is
explanation: Clay minerals are predominantly composed of hydrous aluminium silicates, often with varying amounts of other elements like magnesium, iron, and potassium.

subject: Science & Tech
text: The mineral containing both magnesium and calcium is
explanation: Dolomite is a carbonate mineral composed of calcium magnesium carbonate, with the chemical formula CaMg(CO₃)₂. It is a common rock-forming mineral.

subject: Science & Tech
text: The metal does not give H 2 on treatment with dilute HCL is
explanation: Metals that are less reactive than hydrogen, such as silver (Ag), do not react with dilute acids like HCl to displace hydrogen and produce hydrogen gas. Zinc, iron, and calcium are all more reactive than hydrogen.

subject: Science & Tech
text: The number of g-molecule of oxygen in 6.02 x 10 24 CO molecules is
explanation: 6.02 x 10²⁴ CO molecules is equivalent to 10 moles of CO molecules (since 1 mole = 6.02 x 10²³ molecules). Each CO molecule contains one oxygen atom, so there are 10 moles of oxygen atoms. If 'g-molecule of oxygen' refers to moles of diatomic oxygen (O₂), then 10 moles of oxygen atoms correspond to 5 moles of O₂ molecules.

subject: Science & Tech
text: The most extensive, commercially useful source of thorium as monazite sand occurs in India at
explanation: The Travancore coast in Kerala, India, is renowned for its extensive deposits of monazite sand, which is a commercially significant source of thorium.

subject: Science & Tech
text: The main active constituent of tea and coffee is
explanation: Caffeine is the main active chemical constituent found in both tea and coffee, responsible for their stimulating effects.

subject: Science & Tech
text: The maximum number of isomers for an alkene with molecular formula C 4 H 8 is
explanation: For the molecular formula C₄H₈, the possible alkene isomers are but-1-ene, cis-but-2-ene, trans-but-2-ene, and 2-methylpropene, totaling four distinct structures.

subject: Science & Tech
text: The hardest form of carbon is
explanation: Diamond is an allotrope of carbon where each carbon atom is covalently bonded to four other carbon atoms in a tetrahedral lattice, making it the hardest known natural material.

subject: Science & Tech
text: The organic reaction represented by equation CH 3 - CH = O + H 2 NOH gives CH 3 - CH - NH + H 2 O is an example of
explanation: This reaction is a condensation reaction because two molecules (acetaldehyde and hydroxylamine) combine to form a larger molecule (an oxime) with the elimination of a small molecule, water.

subject: Science & Tech
text: The number of electrons presents in H + is
explanation: H+ is a hydrogen ion, and it is an ion because it LOST an electron. Electrons are negatively charged. When an atom gains electrons it will have NEGATIVE charges. So it has zero electron.

subject: Science & Tech
text: The hottest part of the gas flame is known as
explanation: The non-luminous zone, also known as the blue zone, is where complete combustion occurs due to an adequate supply of oxygen, making it the hottest part of the flame.

subject: Science & Tech
text: The human body is made up of several chemical elements; the element present in the highest proportion (65%) in the body is
explanation: Oxygen is the most abundant element in the human body by mass, accounting for approximately 65%, largely due to its presence in water (H2O).

subject: Science & Tech
text: The isomerism which exists between CH 3 CHCI 2 and CH 2 CI. CH 2 CI is
explanation: Both compounds have the same molecular formula (C2H4Cl2) but differ in the position of the chlorine atoms on the carbon chain, which is the definition of positional isomerism.

subject: Science & Tech
text: The half life period of an isotope is 2 hours. After 6 hours what fraction of the initial quantity of the isotope will be left behind?
explanation: After one half-life (2 hours), 1/2 remains. After three half-lives (6 hours), (1/2)^3 = 1/8 of the initial quantity will be left.

subject: Science & Tech
text: The number of waves made by an electron moving in an orbit having maximum magnetic quantum number is +3
explanation: If the maximum magnetic quantum number (ml) is +3, then the azimuthal quantum number (l) is 3. The principal quantum number (n) must be at least l+1, so n ≥ 4, and the number of waves is equal to n.

subject: Science & Tech
text: The number of atoms present in 21.6 gram of silver (atomic weight = 108) are same as the molecules in
explanation: 21.6 grams of silver corresponds to 0.2 moles of atoms. As written, none of the options contain 0.2 moles of molecules; however, if option B were '0.2 moles of KMnO4', it would be the correct match.

subject: Science & Tech
text: The National Chemical Laboratory is situated in
explanation: The National Chemical Laboratory (NCL) is a constituent laboratory of the Council of Scientific & Industrial Research (CSIR) and is located in Pune, Maharashtra, India.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Equal masses of oxygen, hydrogen and methane are kept under identical conditions. The ratio of the volumes of gases will be
explanation: According to Avogadro's Law, the volume of a gas is proportional to its number of moles. For equal masses (m), the moles are m/MolarMass, leading to a volume ratio of 1:16:2 for O2 (32), H2 (2), and CH4 (16).

subject: Science & Tech
text: The mass number of an atom is equal to
options: the number of protons and electrons
explanation: The mass number (A) of an atom represents the total count of protons and neutrons in its nucleus. Protons and neutrons are collectively referred to as nucleons.

subject: Science & Tech
text: The maximum number of covalent formed by nitrogen is
explanation: Nitrogen typically forms three covalent bonds, but it can form a maximum of four covalent bonds by utilizing its lone pair to form a dative (coordinate) bond, as exemplified in the ammonium ion (NH4+).

subject: Science & Tech
text: The formula C 6 H 5 -CO-CH 3 represents
explanation: The formula C6H5-CO-CH3 consists of a phenyl group (C6H5) attached to an acetyl group (-CO-CH3), which is the structure for acetophenone.

subject: Science & Tech
text: The metal that is usually extracted from sea water is
explanation: Magnesium is the only metal among the options that is commercially extracted from seawater, primarily through precipitation as magnesium hydroxide followed by conversion to magnesium chloride and electrolysis.

subject: Geography
text: The groundwater can become confined between two impermeable layers. This type of enclosed water is called
explanation: Groundwater that is trapped between two impermeable layers is specifically known as confined groundwater. An artesian aquifer is a type of confined aquifer where the water is under pressure.

subject: Geography
text: The largest glaciers are
explanation: The largest glacial bodies, ice sheets or continental glaciers, cover more than 50,000 km 2 (20,000 mile 2 ). Several kilometers deep, they obscure the underlying topography. Only nunataks protrude from the surface. The only extant ice sheets are the two that cover most of Antarctica and Greenland. These regions contain vast quantities of fresh water. The volume of ice is so large that if the Greenland ice sheet melted, it would cause sea levels to rise six meters (20 ft) all around the world. If the Antarctic ice sheet melted, sea levels would rise up to 65 meters (210 ft).

subject: Geography
text: The ionosphere includes
explanation: The ionosphere is a region of the upper atmosphere that is ionized by solar radiation, extending from about 60 km to 1,000 km in altitude, thus encompassing parts of the mesosphere, thermosphere, and exosphere.

subject: Geography
text: The highest degree of concentration of mineral deposits are found in
explanation: In India, the northeastern zone of the Peninsular plateau, particularly the Chota Nagpur Plateau, is renowned for its rich and concentrated deposits of various minerals like coal, iron ore, and mica.

subject: Geography
text: The group of minerals chemically containing hydrocarbons is
explanation: Minerals that chemically contain hydrocarbons, such as coal, petroleum, and natural gas, are classified under the organic group of minerals due to their carbon-hydrogen composition.

subject: Geography
text: The iron ore mined at Bailadila is mostly
explanation: Bailadila, located in Chhattisgarh, India, is renowned for its extensive deposits of high-grade hematite iron ore, which is a primary source for steel production.

subject: Geography
text: The leading state in producing paper is
explanation: West Bengal has historically been a significant state in paper production in India, with several major paper mills located within the state.

subject: Geography
text: The largest dune files are found in
explanation: The largest and most extensive dune fields, known as ergs, are predominantly found in the vast desert regions of North Africa (e.g., Sahara Desert) and the Middle East (e.g., Arabian Desert).

subject: Geography
text: The Harmattan is
options: cool, extremely dry wind that forms over the Sahara and blows westward or south-westward to the African coast
wind that blows during the dry season from December to February
tertiary wind that carry great quantities of fine dust from the Sahara
explanation: The Harmattan is a dry, dusty trade wind that originates over the Sahara Desert, blowing southwestward across West Africa during the dry season (December to February), and is characterized by its cool temperature and significant dust load.

subject: Geography
text: The largest gold producing country in the world(in 2006) is
explanation: In 2006, South Africa was the world's largest gold-producing country, maintaining a long-standing position as a global leader in gold mining before being surpassed by other nations in subsequent years.

subject: Geography
text: The hydrological cycle is a conceptual model that describes
options: the storage of water between biosphere, hydrosphere, atmosphere and lithosphere
the movement of water between biosphere, atmosphere, lithosphere and hydrosphere
explanation: The hydrological cycle describes the continuous movement of water on, above, and below the Earth's surface, encompassing both the storage of water in various reservoirs and its transfer between the biosphere, atmosphere, lithosphere, and hydrosphere.

subject: Geography
text: The least explosive type of volcano is called
explanation: Basalt plateaus, formed by flood basalts, result from highly fluid, low-viscosity lava flows that spread extensively with minimal explosive activity, making them the least explosive type of volcanic formation.

subject: Geography
text: The largest fish exporting region in the world is
explanation: The Northwest Pacific region (FAO Area 61) consistently ranks as the world's largest capture fishery region, contributing significantly to global fish production and exports due to its vast marine resources and extensive fishing activities.

subject: Geography
text: The largest country of the world by geographical area is
explanation: Russia spans Eastern Europe and Northern Asia, covering over 17 million square kilometers, making it the largest country in the world by land area.

subject: Geography
text: The hot and cold deserts together occupy nearly ____ land area of the world.
explanation: Deserts, encompassing both hot arid regions and cold polar deserts, collectively cover approximately one-third of the Earth's total land area.

subject: Geography
text: The highest average salinity amongst the following seas is reported from
explanation: The Dead Sea has an exceptionally high average salinity of about 34.2%, making it one of the saltiest bodies of water globally, significantly higher than the Red, Black, or Mediterranean Seas.

subject: Geography
text: The Himalayan mountain system belongs to which of the following?
explanation: The Himalayan mountain system is a prime example of fold mountains, formed by the intense compression and uplift of the Earth's crust due to the collision of the Indian and Eurasian tectonic plates.

subject: Geography
text: The heavy day soils that show significant expansion and contraction due to the presence or absence of moisture is called
explanation: Vertisols are characterized by a high content of swelling clays, causing them to expand significantly when wet and contract, forming deep cracks, when dry.

subject: Geography
text: The HBJ Upgradation project, upgraded the capacity of HBJ pipeline from 18.2 MM SC MD to
explanation: The HBJ (Hazira-Bijaipur-Jagdishpur) pipeline, a critical natural gas artery in India, underwent an upgradation project that increased its capacity from 18.2 MM SC MD to 33.4 MM SC MD.

subject: Geography
text: The highest mountains on earth namely Mount Everest, K2, Kanchenjanga are located in
explanation: Mount Everest, K2, and Kanchenjunga, the world's highest peaks, are all situated within the Greater Himalayas (Himadri), the highest and most continuous range of the Himalayan mountain system.

subject: Geography
text: The infrared radiation by sun are strongly absorbed by
explanation: Infrared radiation from the sun is strongly absorbed by both carbon dioxide and water vapor in the Earth's atmosphere, which are key greenhouse gases responsible for trapping heat.

subject: Geography
text: The latitude of a place expresses its angular position relative to the plane of
explanation: Latitude defines a place's angular distance, measured in degrees, north or south from the plane of the equator, which serves as the 0-degree reference line.

subject: Geography
text: The landforms that are influences by several process namely, weathering, erosion, deposition are known as
explanation: Landforms shaped by a combination of various geomorphic processes such as weathering, erosion, and deposition over time are known as polygenetic landforms.

subject: Geography
text: The initial increase of magnetic field in magnetic storms is caused
options: when the shock wave, associated with the gusty solar wind, compresses the magnetosphere
when there is a large decrease in field intensity
when the gust wind itself engulfs the magnetosphere
explanation: The initial increase in the Earth's magnetic field during a magnetic storm is caused by the compression of the magnetosphere by the shock wave from a coronal mass ejection or high-speed solar wind stream.

subject: Geography
text: The island state of Australia is
explanation: Tasmania is Australia's only island state, separated from the mainland by Bass Strait and known for its rugged wilderness and unique wildlife.

subject: Geography
text: The Ice age ended about ____ years ago.
explanation: The last major glacial period, commonly referred to as the Ice Age, ended approximately 11,700 to 14,000 years ago, ushering in the current interglacial period.

subject: Geography
text: The languages used in China is
explanation: While 'Chinese' encompasses many dialects and languages, Mandarin is the official standard. English is widely taught and used in business, education, and tourism across China, making it a significant second language.

subject: Geography
text: The islands with coral covered surfaces in Bay of Bengal are
explanation: Both the Andaman and Nicobar island groups in the Bay of Bengal are home to extensive and diverse coral reefs, which contribute significantly to their marine biodiversity.

subject: Geography
text: Within the transporting medium, transport can occur by
explanation: Within a transporting medium like water or wind, materials can be moved by suspension (fine particles carried within the fluid), solution (dissolved substances), and traction (larger particles rolled or dragged along the bed).

subject: Geography
text: The length of the day is determined in
explanation: The length of a day is determined by the Earth's rotation relative to the Sun (for a solar day) or distant stars (for a sidereal day), and is measured in units of time such as hours, minutes, and seconds. None of the provided options accurately describe how the length of the day is determined or its units.

subject: Geography
text: The hot, dry wind on the east or leeward side of the Rocky mountains (North America) is called
explanation: The Chinook is a warm, dry, and often strong wind that descends the eastern (leeward) slopes of the Rocky Mountains in North America, causing rapid temperature increases.

subject: Geography
text: The islands of Seychelles are located in the
explanation: The Seychelles, an archipelago nation, is located in the western Indian Ocean, northeast of Madagascar and east of mainland Africa.

subject: Geography
text: The island of Sri Lanka formerly known as
explanation: The island nation of Sri Lanka was historically known as Ceylon until it adopted its current name in 1972.

subject: Geography
text: The highest sand dunes are found is
explanation: The Gobi Desert, particularly its Badain Jaran section in China, is home to some of the world's tallest stationary sand dunes, with some reaching heights exceeding 500 meters.

subject: Geography
text: The greatest variety of animal and plant species is fund in
explanation: Tropical moist forests, particularly rainforests, are Earth's most biodiverse terrestrial ecosystems, harboring an immense variety of animal and plant species due to their stable warm climate and abundant rainfall.

subject: Geography
text: The layer of the earth, immediately below the crust, is called
explanation: Immediately beneath the Earth's crust lies the mantle, which is divided into upper and lower sections. The 'outer mantle' in this context refers to the upper part of the mantle.

subject: Geography
text: The highest mountains in Africa, which is not part of any mountains chain, is
explanation: Mount Kilimanjaro, Africa's highest peak, is a dormant stratovolcano that stands as an isolated massif, meaning it is not part of a larger mountain chain.

subject: Geography
text: The heavier silicates named as 'Sima' or silica + magnesium are most abundant in the
explanation: Sima, composed of silica and magnesium, forms the denser, lower layer of the Earth's crust, particularly abundant in the oceanic crust which underlies the ocean floors.

subject: Geography
text: The imaginary line on the earth's surface, which closely follows the 180 Âº meridian, is called the
explanation: The International Date Line is an imaginary line that largely follows the 180° meridian of longitude, serving as the demarcation where one calendar day ends and the next begins.

subject: Geography
text: The largest part of our hydrosphere is
explanation: The Pacific Ocean is the largest and deepest of Earth's oceanic divisions, containing the vast majority of the planet's surface water and thus forming the largest part of the hydrosphere.

subject: Geography
text: The limit beyond which the stars suffer internal collapse is called the
explanation: The Chandrasekhar limit defines the maximum mass a white dwarf star can have before its core collapses under gravity, leading to a supernova or further collapse into a neutron star or black hole.

subject: Geography
text: The ground water that occurs when flow of the subterranean water is not confined by the presence of impermeable layers is called
explanation: Unconfined groundwater exists in an aquifer where the upper surface of the water (the water table) is not restricted by an overlying impermeable layer, allowing it to rise and fall freely.

subject: Geography
text: The gulf that separates Finland and Sweden in Europe is
explanation: The Gulf of Bothnia is a northern arm of the Baltic Sea that lies between the western coast of Finland and the eastern coast of Sweden.

subject: Geography
text: The largest continent in the world is
explanation: Asia is the largest continent on Earth, covering approximately 30% of the Earth's total land area and home to over half of the world's population.

subject: Geography
text: The landforms that are created by massive earth movements due to plate tectonics are called
explanation: Structural landforms are directly shaped by large-scale earth movements like folding, faulting, and uplift caused by plate tectonics, which determine the underlying geological structure.

subject: Geography
text: The largest production of mica in Asia is from
explanation: India has historically been one of the world's leading producers of mica, particularly sheet mica, making it the largest producer in Asia.

subject: Geography
text: The latitude 'AA' on the map represents the
explanation: Assuming 'AA' refers to the 0° latitude, the Equator is the imaginary line that encircles the Earth midway between the North and South poles, dividing it into the Northern and Southern Hemispheres.

subject: Geography
text: The largest fresh water lake of Africa, area wise is
explanation: Lake Victoria is the largest freshwater lake in Africa by surface area and the second-largest freshwater lake in the world, shared by Tanzania, Uganda, and Kenya.

subject: Geography
text: The important mountains of Europe include
explanation: The Alps, Carpathians, Pyrenees, and Apennines are all prominent mountain ranges located within the continent of Europe.

subject: Geography
text: The last major glacial period began about 2,000,000 years before present and is commonly known as
explanation: The Pleistocene epoch, commonly known as the Ice Age, began approximately 2.58 million years ago and was characterized by multiple cycles of glacial expansion and retreat.

subject: Geography
text: The imaginary axis at which the earth rotates remains inclined at an angle of ____ to the plane of earth's orbit.
explanation: The Earth's axis of rotation is tilted at an angle of 23.5 degrees relative to the perpendicular of its orbital plane, meaning it is inclined at 66.5 degrees (66 1/2°) to the plane of its orbit around the sun.

subject: Geography
text: The ionosphere contains ionized air that protects the earth from
options: the ultraviolet rays of the sun
the infrared rays of the sun
explanation: The ionosphere, as part of the Earth's upper atmosphere, contributes to the protection against falling meteorites by causing them to burn up due to friction before reaching the surface.

subject: Geography
text: The horizontal of soil profile is composed of
options: weathered parent material that is not affected by translocation and organic modification
material affected by translocation & organic modification
explanation: Assuming 'horizontal' refers to the C horizon, it consists of weathered parent material that has undergone minimal alteration from the biological activity, organic modification, and translocation processes that shape the upper soil horizons.

subject: Geography
text: The largest city in Latin America is
explanation: Mexico City is widely considered the largest city in Latin America by population within its city limits.

subject: Geography
text: The highest and lowest values of a weather element observed over the entire period of record are
explanation: Absolute extremes refer to the highest and lowest values of a weather element, such as temperature or precipitation, that have been recorded over the entire period of observation.

subject: Geography
text: The highest mountain peak of Oceania is
explanation: Puncak Jaya, located in Indonesia's Papua province, is the highest mountain peak in Oceania, standing at an elevation of 4,884 meters (16,024 feet).

subject: Geography
text: The length of the tropical years (the time interval between successive occurrences of the spring equinox) is decreasing very slowly as a result of
options: small, progressive changes in the earth's rotational speed
small, progressive change in earth's orbit around the sun
explanation: The slow decrease in the length of the tropical year is primarily influenced by the precession of the equinoxes, which is itself affected by both the Earth's rotational dynamics (creating the equatorial bulge) and the gravitational forces from the Sun and Moon (related to Earth's orbit).

subject: Geography
text: The largest gulf in the world is
explanation: The Gulf of Mexico is recognized as the largest gulf in the world by surface area, bordered by the United States, Mexico, and Cuba.

subject: Geography
text: The hunting and gathering economy can support only
explanation: Hunting and gathering economies are characterized by very low population densities, typically supporting only about 1 person per square kilometer due to the limited food resources available from wild sources.

subject: Geography
text: The import of crude oil and petroleum done from national oil companies of producer countries, which have a net exportable surplus of oil is by
explanation: National oil companies with an exportable surplus of crude oil and petroleum utilize a variety of trading mechanisms, including long-term term contracts for stable supply, as well as term and monthly tenders for more flexible sales.

subject: History
text: First Afghan War took place in
explanation: The First Afghan War, also known as the First Anglo-Afghan War, began in 1839 when the British invaded Afghanistan to restore Shah Shuja Durrani to power.

subject: History
text: Gulf cooperation council was originally formed by
options: Bahrain, Kuwait, Oman, Qatar, Saudi Arabia and United Arab Emirates
explanation: Gulf cooperation council was created on May 25, 1981, the 630-million-acre (2,500,000 km2) Council comprises the Persian Gulf states of Bahrain, Kuwait, Oman, Qatar, Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates. The unified economic agreement between the countries of the Gulf Cooperation Council was signed on November 11, 1981 in Abu Dhabi. These countries are often referred to as The GCC States.

subject: History
text: First China War was fought between
explanation: The First China War, also known as the First Opium War, was fought between Great Britain and the Qing dynasty of China from 1839 to 1842. It was primarily over trade disputes and China's attempts to suppress the opium trade.

subject: History
text: Dumping is
options: selling of goods abroad at a price well below the production cost at the home market price
the process by which the supply of a manufacture's product remains low in the domestic market, which batches him better price
explanation: Dumping in international trade refers to the practice of a country or company exporting a product at a price lower than its domestic price or its cost of production. While regulated by GATT/WTO, it is not universally prohibited but rather subject to anti-dumping duties.

subject: History
text: For the Olympics and World Tournaments, the dimensions of basketball court are
explanation: For international competitions like the Olympics and World Tournaments, the official dimensions of a basketball court are 28 meters in length and 15 meters in width, as stipulated by FIBA rules.

subject: History
text: Federation Cup, World Cup, Allywyn International Trophy and Challenge Cup are awarded to winners of
explanation: The Federation Cup, World Cup, Allywyn International Trophy, and Challenge Cup are all notable tournaments or trophies associated with the sport of volleyball.

subject: History
text: Each year World Red Cross and Red Crescent Day is celebrated on
explanation: World Red Cross and Red Crescent Day is observed annually on May 8th to commemorate the birth anniversary of Henry Dunant, the founder of the International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC).

subject: History
text: Famous sculptures depicting art of love built some time in 950 AD - 1050 AD are
explanation: The Khajuraho temples, built by the Chandela dynasty between 950 AD and 1050 AD, are renowned for their intricate sculptures, many of which depict various forms of love and erotic art.

subject: History
text: Gravity setting chambers are used in industries to remove
explanation: Gravity setting chambers are industrial devices designed to remove larger, heavier suspended particulate matter from gas streams by allowing these particles to settle out due to gravity.

subject: History
text: Guwahati High Court is the judicature of
explanation: The Guwahati High Court serves as the common high court for the states of Assam, Nagaland, Mizoram, and Arunachal Pradesh, exercising jurisdiction over all these regions.

subject: History
text: Friction can be reduced by changing from
explanation: Friction can be significantly reduced by changing the mode of contact from sliding to rolling, as rolling friction is generally much lower than sliding friction. This principle is utilized in wheels and ball bearings.

subject: History
text: During eleventh Antarctic Expedition in Nov. 1991/March 1992 ____ was installed.
explanation: During India's Eleventh Antarctic Expedition (November 1991 - March 1992), a SODAR (SOnic Detection And Ranging) system was installed at the Maitri station to conduct atmospheric boundary layer studies.

subject: History
text: From the following, choose the set in which names of Secretary-Generals of the UNO are arranged in correct chronological order?
options: U Thant, Kurt Waldheim, Dag Hammarskjoeld, Trygve Lie.
Trygve Lie, Dag Hammarskjoeld, U Thant, Kurt Waldheim.
Trygve Lie, U Thant , Dag Hammarskjoeld, Kurt Waldheim.
U Thant, Dag Hammarskjoeld, Trygve Lie, Kurt Waldheim.
explanation: The correct chronological order of these UN Secretary-Generals is Trygve Lie (1946-1952), followed by Dag Hammarskjöld (1953-1961), then U Thant (1961-1971), and finally Kurt Waldheim (1972-1981).

subject: History
text: Fire temple is the place of worship of which of the following religion?
explanation: Fire temples, known as Agiary or Atash Behram, are the sacred places of worship for followers of Zoroastrianism (Parsi Religion), where fire is revered as a symbol of divine purity and wisdom.

subject: History
text: Film and TV institute of India is located at
explanation: The Film and Television Institute of India (FTII), a premier institute for film and television education, is located in Pune, Maharashtra.

subject: History
text: Georgia, Uzbekistan and Turkmenistan became the members of UNO in
explanation: Georgia, Uzbekistan, and Turkmenistan all became member states of the United Nations in 1992, following the dissolution of the Soviet Union.

subject: History
text: Guru Gobind Singh was
options: the 10 th Guru of the Sikhs
founder of Khalsa, the inner council of the Sikhs in 1699
explanation: Guru Gobind Singh was the tenth and final living Sikh Guru, known for founding the Khalsa in 1699 and for his significant contributions to Sikh literature, including the Dasam Granth.

subject: History
text: Hermann Scheer (Germany) received right Livelihood Award in 1999 for
options: his indefatigable work for thepromotion of solar energy worldwide
his long standing efforts to end the impunity of dictators
showing that organic agriculture is a key to both environmental sustainability and food security
explanation: Hermann Scheer, a German politician and advocate, was awarded the Right Livelihood Award in 1999 for his tireless global efforts in promoting solar energy and renewable energy policies.

subject: History
text: Germany signed the Armistice Treaty on ____ and World War I ended
explanation: Germany signed the Armistice Treaty on November 11, 1918, effectively ending World War I. This date is widely commemorated as Armistice Day or Remembrance Day.

subject: History
text: During World War II, when did Germany attack France?
explanation: During World War II, Germany launched its invasion of France and the Low Countries on May 10, 1940, leading to the rapid fall of France.

subject: History
text: Frederick Sanger is a twice recipient of the Nobel Prize for
explanation: Frederick Sanger was awarded the Nobel Prize in Chemistry twice: first in 1958 for determining the structure of the insulin molecule, and again in 1980 for his contributions to methods for determining base sequences in nucleic acids.

subject: History
text: The ozone layer restricts
explanation: The ozone layer in the Earth's stratosphere plays a crucial role in absorbing and restricting most of the Sun's harmful ultraviolet (UV) radiation, protecting life on Earth.

subject: History
text: Eugenics is the study of
options: altering human beings by changing their genetic components
explanation: Eugenics is the study and practice of selectively improving the genetic quality of the human population, historically through selective breeding and, in modern contexts, potentially through genetic alteration. It aims to enhance desirable traits and reduce undesirable ones in humans.

subject: History
text: Euclid was
options: Contributor to the use of deductive principles of logic as the basis of geometry
explanation: Euclid was a renowned Greek mathematician, famous for his seminal work 'Elements,' which established the deductive principles of logic as the foundation of geometry and propounded numerous geometrical theorems.

subject: History
text: Ecology deals with
options: Relation between organisms and their environment
explanation: Ecology is the scientific study of the relationships between living organisms, including humans, and their physical environment, encompassing their interactions and interdependence.

subject: History
text: Filaria is caused by
explanation: Filaria, or lymphatic filariasis, is caused by parasitic worms transmitted to humans through the bites of infected mosquitoes, which act as the primary vector for the disease.

subject: History
text: Goa Shipyard Limited (GSL) was established in
explanation: Goa Shipyard Limited (GSL), a prominent Indian shipbuilding company, was established in 1957, initially under Portuguese administration before being acquired by the Indian government.

subject: History
text: DRDL stands for
options: Department of Research and Development Laboratory
explanation: DRDL stands for Defence Research and Development Laboratory, a premier laboratory of the Defence Research and Development Organisation (DRDO) in India.

subject: History
text: Who was the first Indian Commander-in-Chief (C-in-C) of the Indian Army?
explanation: Field Marshal K.M. Cariappa was the first Indian Commander-in-Chief of the Indian Army, assuming office on January 15, 1949. This day is celebrated as Army Day in India.

subject: History
text: FRS stands for
explanation: FRS stands for Fellow of the Royal Society, an award granted to individuals who have made a substantial contribution to the improvement of natural knowledge.

subject: History
text: Escape velocity of a rocket fired from the earth towards the moon is a velocity to get rid of the
options: Centripetal force due to the earth's rotation
explanation: Escape velocity is the minimum speed required for an object to break free from the gravitational pull of a celestial body, such as Earth, without further propulsion.

subject: History
text: GATT (General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade) is
options: international agreement signed in 1947 between non-communist nations with the object of encouraging international trade unobstructed by tariff barriers
agreement which seeks to achieve its aim by arranging and encouraging bargaining with trade concessions between members
explanation: The General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (GATT) was a multilateral agreement signed in 1947 aimed at reducing barriers to international trade and encouraging free trade among member nations. It operated by facilitating negotiations for tariff reductions and trade concessions.

subject: History
text: Coral reefs in India can be found in
explanation: In India, significant coral reefs are found in the Gulf of Mannar, which includes the region around Rameshwaram, as well as in the Lakshadweep and Andaman & Nicobar Islands.

subject: History
text: For safety, the fuse wire used in the mains for household supply of electricity must be made of metal having
explanation: A fuse wire is designed to protect electrical circuits by melting and breaking the circuit when an excessive current flows through it, which requires it to have a low melting point.

subject: History
text: Golden Temple, Amritsar is India's
options: Both option A and B are correct
explanation: The Golden Temple (Harmandir Sahib) in Amritsar is the holiest shrine in Sikhism and a highly significant Gurdwara, but it is not definitively the largest or the oldest Gurdwara in India.

subject: History
text: During World War I Germany was defeated in the Battle of Verdun on the western front and Romania declared war on the eastern front in the year
explanation: The Battle of Verdun, a major engagement on the Western Front, occurred in 1916, and Romania declared war on the Eastern Front in August of the same year.

subject: History
text: Heavy Water Project (Talcher) and Fertilizer plant (Paradeep) are famous industries of
explanation: Both the Heavy Water Plant at Talcher and the Fertilizer Plant at Paradeep are significant industrial establishments located in the state of Odisha, India.

subject: History
text: Hamid Karzai was chosen president of Afghanistan in
explanation: Hamid Karzai was chosen as the President of the Afghan Transitional Administration by the Loya Jirga in June 2002, following his appointment as interim leader in December 2001.

subject: History
text: Durand Cup is associated with the game of
explanation: The Durand Cup is an annual football competition in India, making it one of the oldest football tournaments in Asia.

subject: History
text: Headquarters of UNO are situated at
explanation: The primary headquarters of the United Nations Organization (UNO) is located in New York City, USA, while it also maintains major offices in Geneva, Vienna, and Nairobi.

subject: History
text: First International Peace Congress was held in London in
explanation: The first International Peace Congress, a significant gathering of peace activists and organizations, was held in London in 1843.

subject: History
text: For seeing objects at the surface of water from a submarine under water, the instrument used is
explanation: A periscope is the instrument used in submarines to observe objects on the surface of the water, utilizing mirrors or prisms to reflect light from the surface to the observer below.

subject: History
text: Dr. Zakir Husain was
options: the first Muslim president of India
first president of Indian National Congress
explanation: Dr. Zakir Husain served as the third President of India from 1967 to 1969, making him the first Muslim to hold the office of President of India.

subject: History
text: G-15 is an economic grouping of
explanation: The G-15 (Group of Fifteen) is an informal economic grouping of developing countries, often referred to as Third World Nations, formed to enhance cooperation and provide a unified voice on international economic issues.

subject: History
text: Fathometer is used to measure
explanation: A fathometer is an instrument specifically designed to measure the depth of water, primarily by emitting sound waves and calculating the time it takes for the echo to return.

subject: History
text: For galvanizing iron which of the following metals is used?
explanation: Galvanizing iron involves coating it with a layer of zinc to protect it from corrosion and rust, as zinc acts as a sacrificial anode.

subject: History
text: Economic goods are
options: all commodities that are limited in quantity as compared to their demand
Commodities that is available according to their demand
Commodities that is available more as compared to demand
explanation: Economic goods are defined as commodities that are limited in quantity relative to the demand for them, implying scarcity and typically requiring a price for acquisition.

subject: History
text: For purifying drinking water alum is used
explanation: Alum is used in water purification as a coagulant, which helps in the aggregation of fine suspended particles like mud into larger flocs that can then be easily settled or filtered out.

subject: History
text: Hockey was introduced in the Asian Games in
explanation: Field hockey made its debut as a medal sport at the Asian Games in 1958, during the 3rd edition of the games held in Tokyo, Japan.

subject: History
text: ESCAP stands for
options: Economic and Social Commission for Asia and Pacific
European Society Council for Africa and Pacific
Economic and Social Commission for Africa and Pacific
explanation: ESCAP stands for the United Nations Economic and Social Commission for Asia and the Pacific, which is one of the five regional commissions of the United Nations.

subject: History
text: Firdausi was
explanation: Firdausi was a renowned Persian poet, celebrated for his monumental epic poem, the 'Shahnameh' (Book of Kings), which is considered the national epic of Iran.

subject: History
text: Himalayan Mountaineering Institute is at
explanation: The Himalayan Mountaineering Institute (HMI), a premier training center for mountaineering, is located in Darjeeling, West Bengal, India.

subject: History
text: Gabriel Daniel Fahrenheit was
explanation: Gabriel Daniel Fahrenheit was a German physicist who developed the mercury thermometer in 1714 and devised the Fahrenheit temperature scale, which is still used today.

subject: History
text: During the first crusade, crusaders reached Jerusalem and captured it in
explanation: The First Crusade successfully captured Jerusalem on July 15, 1099 AD, after a siege, establishing the Kingdom of Jerusalem.

subject: History
text: Dr. Linus Carl Pauling is the only person to have won two Nobel prizes individually for
explanation: Linus Pauling uniquely won two unshared Nobel Prizes: the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1954 and the Nobel Peace Prize in 1962 for his activism against nuclear weapons.

subject: History
text: Free market is
options: a condition in the international market where nations do not impose customs duty or other taxes on import of goods
market where the price of a commodity is determined by free play of the forces of supply and demand
ports that are exempted from payment of customs duty on articles of commerce, primarily to encourage tourism
explanation: A free market is an economic system where the prices of goods and services are determined by the unrestricted interaction of supply and demand, with minimal government intervention.

subject: History
text: East Timor, which became the 191st member of the UN, is in the continent of
explanation: East Timor (Timor-Leste) is a sovereign state located in Southeast Asia, occupying the eastern half of the island of Timor.

subject: History
text: Free surface of a liquid behaves like a sheet and tends to contract to the smallest possible area due to the
explanation: The free surface of a liquid behaves like a stretched elastic membrane due to surface tension, which is caused by the cohesive forces between the liquid molecules pulling them inwards.

subject: History
text: Gopal Krishna Gokhale
options: started as a maths teacher and rose to the position of the principal of Ferguson College, Pune
founded the servants of India Society
served as President of the Indian National Congress in 1905
explanation: Gopal Krishna Gokhale was a prominent Indian political leader and social reformer who served as a maths teacher and principal, founded the Servants of India Society, and presided over the Indian National Congress in 1905.

subject: History
text: GNLF stands for
explanation: GNLF stands for the Gorkha National Liberation Front, a political party in India that advocates for the rights of the Gorkha community.

subject: History
text: Kathak is a classical dance of
explanation: Kathak is one of the eight principal forms of Indian classical dance, originating from the nomadic bards of ancient North India known as Kathakars or storytellers.

subject: History
text: The head quarters of Sahitya Akademi is at
explanation: The Sahitya Akademi, India's National Academy of Letters, is headquartered in New Delhi and is dedicated to promoting Indian literature.

subject: History
text: The dance encouraged and performance from the temple of Thanjavur was
explanation: Bharatanatyam is a classical Indian dance form that originated in the temples of Tamil Nadu, with a strong historical connection to the Thanjavur region.

subject: History
text: The last Mahakumbh of the 20th century was held at
explanation: The last Mahakumbh Mela of the 20th century was held in Haridwar in 1998, one of the four sacred sites where this Hindu pilgrimage takes place.

subject: History
text: The National Song of India was composed by
explanation: The National Song of India, "Vande Mataram," was composed by Bankim Chandra Chatterji and is a hymn to the Motherland.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Who perfected GENETIC ENGINEERING?
explanation: Genetic Engineering, the technique of DNA cloning was perfected by Stanley Cohen and Herbert Boyer in 1973.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What now-ubiquitous device was invented by Zenith engineer Eugene Polley in 1955?
explanation: The first wireless remote control for television was called Flashmatic. (Remember when you had to get up to change the channel?)

subject: Science & Tech
text: What Benjamin Franklin invented?
explanation: Benjamin Franklin invented bifocal spectacles around 1760, combining lenses for both near and far vision into a single frame to avoid constantly switching glasses.

subject: Science & Tech
text: This part-time race car driver invented the bucket seat in 1969. Who was he??
explanation: While Steve McQueen was a famous actor and racing enthusiast, there is no historical evidence that he invented the bucket seat in 1969. Bucket seats were developed much earlier and were common in cars before this time.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What plaything was invented by Joe McVicker in 1956?
explanation: The stuff was originally intended to be wallpaper cleaner! (I used to eat this as a child)

subject: Science & Tech
text: Who was the first American female to patent her invention, a method of weaving straw with silk?
explanation: Mary Kies was the first American woman to be granted a U.S. patent, which she received in 1809 for her method of weaving straw with silk.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Who invented Gramophone?
explanation: Thomas Alva Edison invented the phonograph in 1877, the first device capable of recording and reproducing sound. While Emile Berliner later invented the gramophone, which used flat discs, Edison's phonograph was the foundational invention.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Who invented Internal Combustion Engine?
explanation: Nikolaus Otto is widely credited with developing the first successful four-stroke internal combustion engine in 1876, which became the basis for most modern engines.

subject: Science & Tech
text: When was Monopoly created?
explanation: After the Stock Market crash, a buying and selling game created by Charles Darrow came to be. The first games were hand drawn on linoleum with streets from Atlantic City, N.J. as the property. He took the game to the Parker Brothers company, and Monopply was brought out for Christmas 1934. Everyone wanted to buy the games after Christmas, and Monopoly became a big success.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What furniture item was invented by California furniture designer Charles Prior Hall in 1968?
explanation: Charles Prior Hall, a California furniture designer, invented the modern waterbed in 1968, initially as a design project.

subject: Science & Tech
text: The Manhattan Project was started by President Roosevelt in 1942 to ensure that the U.S. beat the Germans in developing a nuclear bomb. Whom did Roosevelt appoint as scientific head the Manhattan Project?
explanation: Leslie R. Groves, however, was the military head of the project and the overall supremo, while Robert Oppenheimer was the scientific director. The world's first atomic bomb was exploded in the New Mexico desert on July 16, 1945.

subject: Science & Tech
text: When was the first lawn mower invented?
explanation: The first lawn mowers were not engine powered. Invented in 1830 by Edwin Budding of England, the mowers were turning blades on wooden sticks used to cut grass. Engine powered mowers were not invented until 1919 by an American Army colonel, who used the motor from a washing machine.

subject: Science & Tech
text: In which decade were the first successful diode and triode vacuum tubes invented?
explanation: In 1904, John Ambrose Fleming invented the first practical electron tube called the 'Fleming Valve', which is a diode rectifier. In 1906, Lee de Forest invented the audion later called the triode, which provided signal amplification.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Who is the English physicist responsible for the 'Big Bang Theory'?
explanation: George Gamow, a Russian-American theoretical physicist, was a key proponent and developer of the Big Bang theory, particularly his work on Big Bang nucleosynthesis in the 1940s. He predicted the existence of cosmic microwave background radiation.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What 'game' was first produced by the Southern Novelty Company in Baltimore, Maryland in 1892?
explanation: The Ouija board was first marketed in 1890 by the Kennard Novelty Company in Baltimore, Maryland. It was patented by Elijah Bond, and later popularized by William Fuld.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Who had an explosive idea and first patented DYNAMITE?
explanation: Alfred Nobel patented dynamite in 1867. He later established the Nobel Prizes with his fortune, recognizing those who have conferred the greatest benefit to humankind.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Who invented the Spinning Jenny?
explanation: James Hargreaves invented the Spinning Jenny, a multi-spindle spinning frame, around 1764 and patented it in 1770. This invention significantly improved the efficiency of cotton spinning.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Which insurance salesman invented the fountain pen in 1884?
explanation: Lewis Edson Waterman patented the first practical fountain pen in 1884.

subject: Science & Tech
text: When was the first elevator built?
explanation: The first elevator was built in 1743 for King Louis XV at the Palace of Versailles. It was a counterweighted system that only traveled up one floor.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What charge card, developed in 1950, was the first to be accepted nationally in the US?
explanation: The Diner's Club card, introduced in 1950 by Frank McNamara and Ralph Schneider, was the first multi-purpose charge card accepted nationally in the US.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What Enrico Fermi invented?
explanation: Enrico Fermi led the team that achieved the first self-sustaining nuclear chain reaction in Chicago Pile-1 in 1942, which was the precursor to the nuclear reactor.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Which of the following was first patented by an Australian in 1889?
explanation: The Electric Drill was the invention of Arthur James Arnot. On 20 August 1889 he patented his design while working for the Union Electric Company in Melbourne. His original design was meant primarily for drilling rock to find coal!

subject: Science & Tech
text: Who among the following invented the small pox vaccine?
explanation: Edward Jenner, an English physician, developed the world's first vaccine, for smallpox, in 1796. His work laid the foundation for immunology.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What George Westinghouse invented?
explanation: George Westinghouse invented the railway air brake in 1868, a crucial safety device that allowed trains to stop more reliably and efficiently.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Who is the only U.S. President to invent and patent something?
explanation: In 1849, Abraham Lincoln patented 'A Device for Buoying Vessels Over Shoals', making him the only U.S. President to hold a patent.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What invention is credited to the Russian born American inventor Vladimir Kosma Zworykin?
explanation: Zworykin invented both the iconoscope (television camera tube) for transmission and the kinescope (cathode-ray tube receiver) in 1923 and 1924, respectively, pioneering modern television.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Benjamin Franklin was a prolific inventor. He invented the lightning rod, the Franklin stove, studied electricity, discovered the Gulf Stream, started the first library, and on and on. Among his many other inventions, what musical instrument did he invent?
explanation: Benjamin Franklin invented the glass armonica in 1761, a musical instrument made of rotating glass bowls. He did not invent the harmonium, which is a type of free-reed organ developed later.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What Thomas Davenport invented?
explanation: Thomas Davenport invented the first American DC electric motor in 1834 and used it to power a small model electric car, demonstrating the potential for electric traction, which was a precursor to the electric streetcar.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What Elisha Otis invented?
options: The brake used in modern elevators
explanation: In 1853, American inventor Elisha Otis demonstrated a freight elevator equipped with a safety device to prevent falling in case a supporting cable should break. This increased public confidence in such devices. Otis established a company for manufacturing elevators and patented (1861) a steam elevator. Many people think that he invented the elevator, but the truth is, he invented the elevator brake. He also invented the railway safety brake.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Where was the yo-yo invented?
explanation: The yo-yo's origins are traced back to ancient Greece, but the modern toy is strongly associated with the Philippines, where it was used as a weapon and later popularized as a toy. Donald Duncan popularized it in the U.S. after seeing it there in the 1920s.

subject: Science & Tech
text: For what does the world owe Almon Strowger a debt of gratitude?
explanation: Almon Strowger, an undertaker, invented the automatic telephone exchange (the Strowger switch) in 1889, revolutionizing telephone communication by eliminating the need for human operators.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Who is often called the 'first African-American' inventor?
explanation: Benjamin Banneker, an almanac author, surveyor, and naturalist, is often recognized as one of the earliest African-American scientists and inventors. He was also a member of the planning committee that designed Washington, D.C.

subject: Science & Tech
text: The first hand glider was designed by...?
explanation: Leonardo da Vinci designed several flying machines, including early concepts for gliders, in the late 15th and early 16th centuries, though they were not built or tested in his lifetime.

subject: Science & Tech
text: In which decade was the telegraph invented?
explanation: On May 24, 1844, Samuel Morse sent his famous message, "What hath God wrought?" marking the first successful long-distance telegraph transmission, placing the invention in the 1840s.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What inspired reflecting road lights to be invented?
options: The light a cat's eyes gave off on a fence
Sun light on steel posts on road sides
The sun light on the windshield
explanation: In 1933, Englishman Percy Shaw was inspired to invent 'cat's eyes' (road reflectors) after noticing the reflective glow of a cat's eyes in his car's headlights on a foggy night.

subject: Science & Tech
text: When were blue jeans invented?
explanation: Blue jeans, specifically riveted denim work pants, were patented by Levi Strauss and Jacob Davis in 1873. Levi Strauss had started his dry goods business in the 1850s, but the iconic jeans were developed later.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Who invented Electric Generator?
explanation: Michael Faraday invented the first electromagnetic generator, known as the 'Faraday disk,' in 1831, demonstrating the principle of electromagnetic induction.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What device for new parents did Ann Moore invent in 1969?
explanation: Ann got the idea for the Snugli infant carrier from the traditional African baby carriers she observed while a volunteer for the Peace Corps in Africa.

subject: Science & Tech
text: In which decade was the transistor invented?
explanation: On December 23, 1947, William Shockley, Walter Brattain and John Bardeen, of Bell Labs, announced their discovery of the point-contact germanium transistor to management.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What Igor Sikorsky invented?
explanation: Igor Sikorsky designed and flew the VS-300, the first practical single-rotor helicopter, in 1939. His company, Sikorsky Aircraft, went on to produce the first mass-produced helicopters.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What Henry Bessemer invented?
explanation: Henry Bessemer invented the Bessemer process, a method for mass-producing steel from molten pig iron. This revolutionary process, developed in the 1850s, significantly reduced the cost of steel production.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What invention was first installed at a Hartford, Connecticut bank in 1889?
explanation: William Gray, inventor of the pay telephone, also invented the inflatable chest protector worn by baseball umpires and catchers.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Ralph Samuelson was only 18 when he invented this sporting item in 1922. What was that?
explanation: Ralph Samuelson, at 18, invented water skis in 1922 by using two boards as skis and a clothesline as a tow rope. He performed the first water ski ride on Lake Pepin in Minnesota.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Who invented Bifocal Lens?
explanation: Benjamin Franklin invented bifocal lenses in the late 18th century to avoid constantly switching between two pairs of glasses. He combined two different lenses into one frame, with the reading lens at the bottom and the distance lens at the top.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What Karl Benz invented?
explanation: Karl Benz invented the Benz Patent-Motorwagen in 1885, which is widely regarded as the first practical automobile powered by a gasoline engine. He received the patent for his invention in 1886.

subject: Science & Tech
text: When were bar code scanners invented?
explanation: The first commercial barcode scanner was used in a supermarket in 1974, marking the beginning of their widespread adoption in retail. The underlying barcode technology itself was patented earlier, in 1952.

subject: Science & Tech
text: When was the game Frisbee invented?
explanation: The first Frisbees were pie tins from the Frisbie Pie Company in Connecticut. In the 1950s, the pie tins were changed to plastic plates. The name then became the Flying Saucer, but later changed to Frisbee.

subject: Science & Tech
text: This English inventor is known as the 'Father of Computing.'
explanation: Mauchly and Eckert invented the ENIAC computer. Farnsworth was a pioneer of early television.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Who invented Airship (rigid)?
explanation: Count Ferdinand von Zeppelin designed and built the first successful rigid airship, the LZ 1, which first flew in 1900. These airships, known as Zeppelins, revolutionized long-distance air travel in the early 20th century.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What piece of clothing was invented by French fashion designer Louis Reard in 1946?
explanation: The bikini, a two-piece swimsuit named for the Bikini Atoll in the Pacific, made its debut on July 5, 1946.

subject: Science & Tech
text: In which year was MIDI introduced?
explanation: MIDI (Musical Instrument Digital Interface) was introduced in 1983 as a technical standard that allows electronic musical instruments, computers, and other audio devices to communicate with each other. It revolutionized music production and performance.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Who invented the first controllable flying AEROPLANE (AIRPLANE)?
explanation: Wilbur and Orville Wright made the first successful controlled powered flight at Kitty Hawk, North Carolina, in December 1903. The twelve second flight covered a distance of 120 feet and an altitude of just ten feet.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What groovy item was invented by Englishman Edward Craven Walker in 1963?
explanation: Groovy, baby! Lava lamps, which were a fixture in every hip pad in the 1960s and 70s, featured a mixture of water and colored oil that created swirl patterns when heated by a light bulb.

subject: Science & Tech
text: In which decade was the Internet first implemented?
explanation: The first message ever sent over what is now called the Internet took place at 10:30PM on October 29, 1969. Back then, the Department of Defense called it ARPAnet (Advanced Research Projects Agency network).

subject: Science & Tech
text: What did cardiologist Dr. Paul Zoll invent in Massachusetts in 1952?
explanation: Dr. Paul Zoll invented the first external cardiac pacemaker in 1952, which used electrical impulses to stimulate the heart. His work was crucial in developing devices to regulate heart rhythm and save lives.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What Elmer A. Sperry invented?
explanation: Elmer A. Sperry invented the gyrocompass, a non-magnetic compass that uses a rapidly spinning rotor to find true north. His invention, patented in 1908, was vital for accurate navigation on ships and aircraft.

subject: Science & Tech
text: For over 500 years, paper was only available and sold as single sheets. In 1902, an inventive Australian used half size sheets of paper, a bit of glue and cardboard to create the what?
explanation: Birchall's of Launceston in Tasmania started selling the world’s first notepads, calling them 'Silvercity Writing Tablets' after J A Birchall thought it would be a good idea to back sheets of paper with cardboard and glue them together at the top. His British paper suppliers weren’t so keen, but, they were persuaded by Birchall that it was a good idea!

subject: Science & Tech
text: Who developed the idea of Crop Rotation?
explanation: Viscount Charles 'Turnip' Townsend developed this revolutionary concept.

subject: Science & Tech
text: CORN FLAKES - Who made them first?
explanation: Will Keith Kellogg discovered the corn flake, by mistake, in 1906. They were the result of an experiment which went wrong.

subject: Science & Tech
text: William Frederick is credited with the invention of the modern frisbee in the mid 1950's. In 1957 the Wham-O Company bought his idea and the rest is history. They named the toy after William Frisbie who was a ________?
explanation: Frisbie made a brand of pies that were popular with Yale students in the 1870's. After eating the pies they would throw the pie tins through the air.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Who came up with the idea for INSTANT MASHED POTATO?
explanation: Instant mash was invented by Edward A Asselbergs, a Canadian, in 1962. The mash is actually dehydyated potato flakes.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Who invented Dynamite?
explanation: Alfred Nobel invented dynamite in 1867, a safer and more stable explosive than nitroglycerin. His invention revolutionized mining, construction, and demolition, and he later used his fortune to establish the Nobel Prizes.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What toy did George Lerner create for Hasbro company in 1952?
explanation: George Lerner invented Mr. Potato Head, which was first manufactured by Hasbro in 1952. It featured a plastic potato-shaped body onto which various facial features and accessories could be attached.

subject: Science & Tech
text: For whom high heeled shoes were invented?
explanation: King Louis XIV was very short and didn't like looking up at people he spoke to, so he had a tall pair of shoes made for him.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What African-American inventor received 5 patents in the field of shoemaking?
explanation: Jan Ernst Matzeliger invented a shoe-lasting machine that significantly automated and sped up the process of attaching the upper part of a shoe to its sole, revolutionizing shoemaking.

subject: Science & Tech
text: It travels over land and water. Who invented the HOVERCRAFT?
explanation: Christopher Cockerell invented the hovercraft in 1956. His early experiments with the idea involved a cat food tin, a coffee tin and an industrial blower.

subject: Science & Tech
text: In which decade was the AEEE (now the IEEE) founded?
explanation: The IEEE (Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers) was formed in 1963 by the merger of the Institute of Radio Engineers (IRE, founded 1912) and the American Institute of Electrical Engineers (AIEE, founded 1884).

subject: Science & Tech
text: What type of system did Paul Nipkow, John Baird, and Charles Jenkins all invent?
explanation: All of the inventors mentioned invented an early mechanical television system.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What Sir Isaac Newton invented?
explanation: Sir Isaac Newton designed and built the first functional reflecting telescope in 1668, using mirrors instead of lenses to avoid chromatic aberration.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Who invented Bicycle?
explanation: Kirkpatrick Macmillan, a Scottish blacksmith, is widely credited with inventing the first pedal-driven bicycle around 1839, allowing riders to propel it without touching their feet to the ground.

subject: Science & Tech
text: In the 1700s, who would pay the postage fee for letters before the stamp was invented?
explanation: Later in 1840, Rowland Hill invented the stamp, which was paid for by the sender, not the receiver.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Who synthesized the first wholly artificial gene?
explanation: Har Gobind Khorana, an Indian-American biochemist, was awarded the Nobel Prize for his work on genetic code and for synthesizing the first wholly artificial gene in 1970.

subject: Science & Tech
text: FROZEN FOOD - Who discovered the first practical method of freezing food?
explanation: In the 1920s Clarence Birdseye discovered that if food was flash-frozen under pressure, it would freeze without the formation of ice crystals.

subject: Science & Tech
text: When was the Raggedy Ann doll patented?
explanation: Johnny Gruelle patented the Raggedy Ann doll in 1915. The Raggedy Andy doll and the Raggedy Ann books came later.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What Frank Whittle invented?
explanation: Sir Frank Whittle, an English aviation engineer, is credited with inventing the turbojet engine, a key component of jet propulsion, in 1937.

subject: Science & Tech
text: When was the first Lionel trains manufactured?
explanation: Joshua Lionel Cowen and Harry C. Grant started the Lionel Manufacturing Company on Sept. 5, 1900 in New York City. The first train, which was called the Electric Express, was manufactured in 1901 and was an open gondola propelled by a fan motor. As time has passed, the trains have been made in different colors and styles.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What item, originally called the 'Whirlwind', was invented by Ives McGaffey in 1869?
explanation: Ives McGaffey invented the 'Whirlwind' in 1869, which was a hand-cranked device considered one of the earliest forms of the vacuum cleaner.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What office item was invented by Bette Nesmith Graham in 1951?
explanation: Bette Nesmith Graham, a secretary, invented Liquid Paper (correction fluid) in 1951 to correct typing errors, which became an indispensable office item.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What George Pullman invented?
explanation: George Pullman invented and developed the Pullman sleeping car in 1857, revolutionizing overnight rail travel with comfortable and luxurious accommodations.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Who invented Bakelite?
explanation: Leo Hendrik Baekeland invented the first plastic called Bakelite.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What toy was invented by Joshua Lionel Cowen around 1900?
explanation: Joshua Lionel Cowen founded the Lionel Manufacturing Company in 1900 and manufactured his first electric train, the 'Electric Express', in 1901.

subject: Science & Tech
text: This African-American woman physical therapist worked with soldiers disabled in World War II. She invented a device that helped the disabled to eat by delivering food through a tube to a mouthpiece.
explanation: Bessie Blount, an African-American physical therapist, invented an electronic feeding device in 1948 that allowed disabled patients to feed themselves by delivering food through a tube to a mouthpiece.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What invention by Garnet Carter made its debut at the Fairyland Inn Resort in Lookout Mountain, Tennessee, in 1927?
explanation: It was originally called 'Tom Thumb Golf'.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Who invented the phonograph?
explanation: Thomas Edison is widely credited with inventing the phonograph in 1877. John Kruesi, a skilled machinist working for Edison, built the first working prototype based on Edison's design.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What beverage was invented by Charles Alderton in 1885 in Waco, Texas?
explanation: The cherry-flavored cola made its debut at Morrison's Old Corner Drugstore.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Who invented fuel cells in 1839?
explanation: Fuel cells were invented in 1839 by Sir William Grove.

subject: Science & Tech
text: The ELECTRIC BATTERY, who's charged with inventing this one?
explanation: Italian, Alessandro Volta discovered the first practical battery in 1800. Life would be very different without batteries.

subject: Science & Tech
text: In which decade was the ARRL founded?
explanation: On April 6, 1914, Hiram Percy Maxim proposed the formation of the American Radio Relay League.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Who invented the Seed Drill?
explanation: Jethro Tull, an English agricultural pioneer, invented an improved seed drill in 1701 that significantly increased crop yields by planting seeds efficiently.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What drink was invented by Charles Leiper Grigg in 1929?
explanation: Grigg, who was from Missouri, originally named his creation 'Bib-Label Lithiated Lemon-Lime Soda'.

subject: Science & Tech
text: For what is Charles Babbage (1792-1871) remembered?
explanation: Known as the Father of Computing, Charles Babbage designed the Analytical Engine in 1833, which is considered the first mechanical general-purpose computer, though he never completed it.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Who invented Automobiles using gasoline?
explanation: Karl Benz is credited with inventing the first practical automobile powered by a gasoline engine in 1886, known as the Benz Patent-Motorwagen.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Who invented the battery?
explanation: He was an Italian scientist, and he invented the battery in 1800.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Where were wigs first invented?
explanation: As you have noticed in pictures, many Egyptians have head coverings, sometimes with beads in them. The first wigs were created to cover the head from the hot sun.

subject: Science & Tech
text: Who was the first person to patent the JET ENGINE?
explanation: British inventor Frank Whittle was the first to patent his design for a jet engine in 1930. Although an engine designed by Dr. Hans Von Ohain was the first to fly in 1939. Whittle's jet engine first flew in 1941.

subject: Science & Tech
text: The initials JCB on earth-moving equipment are those of the founder of the company that makes it. What is his name?
explanation: Joseph Cyril Bamford founded the company that makes these iconic machines in 1945, in a 12ft X 15ft garage in Staffordshire.

subject: Science & Tech
text: What treat was invented by 11-year-old Frank Epperson in 1905?
explanation: It was originally called the 'Epsicle' in his honor. (I can see why that name didn't catch on).

subject: Science & Tech
text: When was Milk Tray first introduced?
explanation: Milk Tray was introduced in 1915 in open boxes arranged on wooden trays. Today, with its distinctive purple and gold cartons and popularised by the "Man in Black" television adverts, its one of the most popular chocolate assortments.

subject: History
text: The name of Pierre Cardin is associated with which of the following fields?
explanation: Pierre Cardin was a celebrated French fashion designer known for his avant-garde style and geometric designs, particularly in the 1960s.

subject: History
text: Who is well known collector of artefacts and curios?
explanation: Salar Jung III, Mir Yousuf Ali Khan, was a prime minister of Hyderabad and an avid art collector whose vast collection forms the basis of the renowned Salar Jung Museum.

subject: History
text: Ms. Vijaya Mehta has earned distinction in which of the field?
explanation: Vijaya Mehta is a distinguished Indian theatre and film director, recognized for her significant contributions to experimental theatre and her work with the Marathi stage.

subject: History
text: Dr. M. S. Swaminathan has distinguished himself in which of the following fields?
explanation: Dr. M. S. Swaminathan was an eminent Indian agricultural scientist and geneticist, widely recognized as the 'Father of the Green Revolution in India' for his work in developing high-yield crop varieties.

subject: History
text: Satyen Bose has shown his excellence in which of the following fields?
explanation: Satyen Bose (22 January 1916 - 9 June 1993) was a film director from India. He has directed both Bengali and Hindi films. Among his most notable films are: Raat Aur Din, Chalti Ka Naam Gaadi, Dosti and Jagriti. Jagriti won the Filmfare Best Movie Award in 1956 and Dosti won same award in 1964.

subject: History

subject: History

subject: History

explanation: R. K. Laxman was a celebrated Indian cartoonist, best known for his daily cartoon strip 'You Said It' in The Times of India and his iconic creation, 'The Common Man'.

subject: History

explanation: Samantha Fox is an English singer, songwriter, and former glamour model who rose to fame in the 1980s as a pop music icon.

explanation: Pandit Hari Prasad Chaurasia is a globally celebrated Indian classical musician, renowned for his mastery of the bansuri (flute).

explanation: Graham Greene (1904-1991) was a prominent English novelist and short story writer, celebrated for his significant contributions to 20th-century literature.

subject: History

explanation: M. Fathima Beevi was a distinguished Indian judge who made history as the first female judge appointed to the Supreme Court of India.

subject: History

explanation: Nek Chand Saini (1924-2015) was the visionary creator of the iconic Rock Garden in Chandigarh, an extraordinary sculpture garden made from industrial and home waste.

subject: History

subject: History
text: Who is known as 'Trimurthy of Carnatic Music'?

They were not only contemporaries during the later half of the 18th century and the beginning of the 19th century, but were also, incredibly, born in the same town of Thiruvarur in the Thanjavur district of Tamil Nadu.

subject: History
text: Gangubai hangal is the name associated with
explanation: Gangubai Hangal was a renowned Indian classical vocalist, celebrated for her powerful voice and mastery of the Kirana gharana style of Hindustani classical music.

subject: History
text: Who is known as the 'Lady with the Lamp'?
explanation: Florence Nightingale earned the moniker 'The Lady with the Lamp' for her tireless work and nightly rounds tending to wounded soldiers during the Crimean War. She is considered the founder of modern nursing.

subject: History
text: Girilal Jain was a noted figure in which of the following fields?
explanation: Girilal Jain was a distinguished Indian journalist and intellectual, best known for his long tenure as the editor of The Times of India. He was a respected commentator on national and international affairs.

subject: History
text: Who is Larry Pressler?
explanation: Larry Pressler is an American politician who served as a U.S. Senator and U.S. Representative from South Dakota. He is known for the Pressler Amendment, which restricted aid to Pakistan.

subject: History

subject: History
text: Raja Ravi Verma, was famous in which of the fields?
explanation: Raja Ravi Varma was a renowned Indian painter and artist, famous for his realistic depictions of Hindu deities and mythological scenes. He is considered one of the greatest painters in the history of Indian art.

subject: History
text: Who is known as 'Desert Fox'?

subject: History
text: The title of 'sparrow' given to
explanation: Major General Rajinder Singh, a distinguished officer of the Indian Army, was affectionately known as 'Sparrow' due to his small stature and swift movements.

subject: History

explanation: Dr. S.Z. Qasim (Syed Zahoor Qasim), an eminent Indian marine biologist, led India's first successful scientific expedition to Antarctica in 1981-82.

subject: History

explanation: Yamini Krishnamurthy is a celebrated Indian classical dancer primarily known for her mastery of Bharatanatyam and Kuchipudi, while the other options are renowned Odissi exponents.

subject: History

explanation: Michael Jackson was a globally celebrated American singer, songwriter, and dancer, widely recognized as the 'King of Pop' for his immense contributions to music and popular culture.

subject: History
text: Ms Kim Campbell is the first woman Prime Minister of
explanation: Kim Campbell made history as the first and only woman to serve as Prime Minister of Canada, holding the office in 1993.

subject: History

explanation: Mihir Sen was the first Indian to successfully swim across the English Channel in 1958, achieving a remarkable feat of endurance.

subject: History
text: George Bernard Shaw, the great dramatist, was
explanation: George Bernard Shaw was a celebrated Irish playwright and critic, born in Dublin, Ireland. He is one of the most influential figures in Western theatre.

subject: History
text: 'Mirchchhakatika' was written by

subject: History
text: The famous book 'Anandmath' was authored by

The novel and film are set in the events of the Sannyasi Rebellion, which took place in the late 18th century in eastern India, especially Bengal.

subject: History

subject: History
text: Who is the author of famous statement: "That Government is the best which governs least"?
explanation: Henry David Thoreau 1849 "We've heard it said, that government is best which governs least. That government is best which governs not at all, and when men are prepared for it, that will be the kind of government they will have.

subject: History
text: Which of the following English films was the first one to be dubbed in Hindi?
explanation: 'Universal Soldier' (1992) is widely considered one of the first major Hollywood films to be dubbed and released in Hindi for the Indian theatrical market, preceding 'Aladdin' which was released later the same year.

subject: History
text: Who wrote the book 'Reminiscences of the Nehru Age'?
explanation: M.O. Mathai, who served as the private secretary to India's first Prime Minister Jawaharlal Nehru, authored the book 'Reminiscences of the Nehru Age'. The book offered personal insights into Nehru's life and administration.

subject: History
text: Aurobindo was the author of
explanation: Sri Aurobindo was the author of 'Savitri: A Legend and a Symbol', an epic poem in blank verse. It is considered one of his greatest literary works, embodying his spiritual philosophy.

subject: History
text: Which of the following is a biography of the Lady Mountbatten?
options: Edwina Mountbatten - A Life of Her Own.
Edwina Mountbatten - Her relationship with Nehru
explanation: 'Edwina Mountbatten: A Life of Her Own' by Janet Morgan is a comprehensive biography of Lady Edwina Mountbatten, wife of Lord Mountbatten, detailing her life and significant contributions.

subject: History

subject: History
